title: Design, Modelling and Experimental Evaluation of a Tendon-driven Wrist Abduction-Adduction Mechanism for an upper limb exoskeleton
comment: 8 pages and 8 figures. Submitted to IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics. Includes experimental validation on human participants
summary: Wrist exoskeletons play a vital role in rehabilitation and assistive applications, yet conventional actuation mechanisms such as electric motors or pneumatics often introduce undesirable weight, friction, and complexity. This paper presents a novel single-cable (tendon), torsional-spring-assisted actuation mechanism for wrist abduction-adduction, and a simulation-based method for selecting its stiffness parameters. The mechanism employs a single Bowden cable passively tensioned by a spiral torsional spring (clock spring) to maintain continuous cable tension without antagonistic actuation. Kinematic and dynamic modeling of the mechanism was performed to estimate the required torque and identify optimal spring parameters. These simulation-derived parameters guided the design of a functional prototype, which was experimentally evaluated with five participants with no motor disabilities (NMD) under varying arm positions and loading conditions using three spring configurations to account for user variability and modeling uncertainties. Experimental results show consistent agreement with simulation-derived trends, with the nominal spring configuration achieving balanced motion range, torque demand, and repeatability. The results demonstrate that simulation-informed stiffness selection can effectively guide the design of compact, cable-driven wrist exoskeletons while reducing reliance on empirical tuning.

AI.tldr: A single-cable, torsional-spring-assisted wrist exoskeleton for abduction-adduction is presented, with simulation-guided stiffness selection validated experimentally on five participants.
AI.motivation: Conventional actuation (electric motors, pneumatics) adds undesirable weight, friction, and complexity to wrist exoskeletons, motivating a simpler, lighter cable-driven mechanism.
AI.method: A single Bowden cable passively tensioned by a spiral torsional (clock) spring is used. Kinematic/dynamic modeling estimates required torque and optimal spring parameters, guiding prototype design. Three spring configurations were tested on five NMD participants across varying arm positions and loads.
AI.result: Experimental results matched simulation trends; the nominal spring configuration achieved balanced motion range, torque demand, and repeatability.
AI.conclusion: Simulation-informed stiffness selection effectively guides compact, cable-driven wrist exoskeleton design, reducing reliance on empirical tuning.

title: A Tendon-Driven Wrist Abduction-Adduction Joint Improves Performance of a 5 DoF Upper Limb Exoskeleton -- Implementation and Experimental Evaluation
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures and 1 table. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering as invited IEEE EMBC special issue paper. Under review after first revision
summary: Wrist function is essential in performing activities of daily living (ADLs). However, there is limited experimental evidence on the functional impact of wrist Abduction-Adduction (Ab-Ad) joint assistance in upper limb exoskeletons (ULEs) for rehabilitation. This study evaluates the effect of implementing an active wrist Ab-Ad joint in a five degree of freedom (DoF) ULE, EXOTIC2 exoskeleton, to support individuals with severe motor impairments. Methods: A compact, lightweight wrist module with tendon-driven abduction and spring-driven adduction was integrated into the EXOTIC exoskeleton. Eight adults with no motor disabilities completed drinking and scratching tasks under randomized wrist-enabled and wrist-locked conditions along with a preliminary feasibility test in one individual with Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). Kinematic and task performance metrics including wrist range of motion, task completion time, spillage and leveling metrics were assessed. Results: Implementing the wrist Ab-Ad DoF improved task success metrics. Spill incidence during the drinking task decreased from 56% to 3%, and leveling success for scratching task improved from 28% to 75%. Conclusion: Integrating wrist Ab-Ad assistance improved key functional task outcomes without increasing execution time. Significance: The study provides the experimental evidence that active wrist Ab-Ad control enhances task-level performance in exoskeleton-assisted ADLs.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates the addition of an active wrist Abduction-Adduction (Ab-Ad) joint to the EXOTIC2 exoskeleton, showing significant improvements in task success (spillage reduced from 56% to 3% in drinking; leveling success from 28% to 75% in scratching) without increasing task time.
AI.motivation: Wrist function is critical for ADLs, yet there is limited experimental evidence on the functional benefits of wrist Ab-Ad assistance in upper limb exoskeletons for rehabilitation.
AI.method: A compact, lightweight wrist module (tendon-driven abduction, spring-driven adduction) was integrated into the 5-DoF EXOTIC exoskeleton. Eight healthy adults and one ALS individual performed drinking and scratching tasks under randomized wrist-enabled vs. wrist-locked conditions.
AI.result: Wrist Ab-Ad DoF significantly improved task performance: spill incidence dropped from 56% to 3%, and scratching leveling success rose from 28% to 75%, without increasing execution time.
AI.conclusion: Integrating active wrist Ab-Ad assistance enhances functional task outcomes in exoskeleton-assisted ADLs, providing experimental evidence for its clinical significance.

title: Clinical Evaluation of a Tongue-Controlled Wrist Abduction-Adduction Assistance in a 6-DoF Upper-Limb Exoskeleton for Individuals with ALS and SCI
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures and 2 tables. This work has been submitted to the IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
summary: Upper-limb exoskeletons (ULEs) have the potential to restore functional independence in individuals with severe motor impairments; however, the clinical relevance of wrist degrees of freedom (DoF), particularly abduction-adduction (Ab-Ad), remains insufficiently evaluated. This study investigates the functional and user-perceived impact of wrist Ab-Ad assistance during two activities of daily living (ADLs). Wrist Ab-Ad assistance in a tongue-controlled 6-DoF ULE, EXOTIC2, was evaluated in a within-subject study involving one individual with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and five individuals with spinal cord injury. Participants performed drinking and scratch stick leveling tasks with EXOTIC2 under two conditions: with and without wrist Ab-Ad assistance. Outcome measure included task success, task completion time, kinematic measures, and a usability questionnaire capturing comfort, functional perception, and acceptance. Enabling wrist Ab-Ad improved task success rates across both ADLs, with consistent reductions in spillage (from 77.8% spillages to 22.2%) and failed placements (from 66.7% to 16.7%). Participants utilized task-specific subsets of the available wrist range of motion, indicating that effective control within functional ranges was more critical than maximal joint excursion. Questionnaire responses indicated no increase in discomfort with the additional DoF and reflected perceived improvements in task performance. In conclusion, wrist Ab-Ad assistance enhances functional task performance in assistive exoskeleton use without compromising user comfort. However, its effectiveness depends on task context, control usability, and individual user strategies. This study provides clinically relevant, user-centered evidence supporting the inclusion of wrist Ab-Ad in ULEs, emphasizing the importance of balancing functional capability with usability in assistive device design.

AI.tldr: This study evaluated wrist abduction-adduction (Ab-Ad) assistance in a tongue-controlled 6-DoF upper-limb exoskeleton (EXOTIC2) with six participants (1 ALS, 5 SCI). Results showed wrist Ab-Ad assistance improved task success rates, reduced spillage (from 77.8% to 22.2%) and failed placements (from 66.7% to 16.7%), without increasing discomfort, supporting its inclusion in assistive exoskeleton design.
AI.motivation: The clinical relevance of wrist abduction-adduction (Ab-Ad) degrees of freedom in upper-limb exoskeletons for severe motor impairments remains insufficiently evaluated.
AI.method: Within-subject study with 6 participants (1 ALS, 5 SCI) using a tongue-controlled 6-DoF ULE (EXOTIC2). Participants performed drinking and scratch stick leveling tasks with and without wrist Ab-Ad assistance. Outcome measures included task success, completion time, kinematics, and usability questionnaire.
AI.result: Wrist Ab-Ad assistance improved task success rates across both ADLs. Spillage reduced from 77.8% to 22.2%, failed placements from 66.7% to 16.7%. Participants used task-specific subsets of wrist range of motion. No increased discomfort reported; perceived improvements in task performance noted.
AI.conclusion: Wrist Ab-Ad assistance enhances functional task performance in assistive exoskeleton use without compromising comfort, though effectiveness depends on task context, control usability, and individual strategies. Provides evidence supporting inclusion of wrist Ab-Ad in ULE design.

title: A Survey of Legged Robotics in Non-Inertial Environments: Past, Present, and Future
summary: Legged robots have demonstrated remarkable agility on rigid, stationary ground, but their locomotion reliability remains limited in non-inertial environments, where the supporting ground moves, tilts, or accelerates. Such conditions arise in ground transportation, maritime platforms, and aerospace settings, and they introduce persistent time-varying disturbances that break the stationary-ground assumptions underlying conventional legged locomotion. This survey reviews the state of the art in modeling, state estimation, and control for legged robots in non-inertial environments. We summarize representative application domains and motion characteristics, analyze the root causes of locomotion performance degradation, and review existing methods together with their key assumptions and limitations. We further identify open problems in robot-environment coupling, observability, robustness, and experimental validation, and discuss future directions in autonomy, system-level design, bio-inspired strategies, safety, and testing. The survey aims to clarify the technical foundations of this emerging area and support the development of reliable legged robots for real-world dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: This survey reviews legged robot locomotion in non-inertial environments (moving, tilting, or accelerating ground), covering modeling, state estimation, and control methods, while identifying open challenges and future directions.
AI.motivation: Legged robots perform well on rigid, stationary ground but fail in non-inertial environments (e.g., moving vehicles, ships, spacecraft) where the supporting surface moves, tilts, or accelerates, causing persistent time-varying disturbances that existing methods cannot handle.
AI.method: The paper surveys representative application domains, analyzes root causes of locomotion degradation, reviews existing methods in modeling, state estimation, and control along with their assumptions and limitations.
AI.result: Identifies open problems in robot-environment coupling, observability, robustness, and experimental validation; summarizes limitations of current approaches under non-inertial conditions.
AI.conclusion: The survey clarifies technical foundations for legged locomotion in dynamic environments and points to future directions in autonomy, system-level design, bio-inspired strategies, safety, and standardized testing.

title: The Radon Transform, True Time Delay Beamforming, and Ultra-Wideband Antenna Arrays (Invited Paper)
comment: Accepted to the IEEE Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems & Computers 2025
summary: The FR3 band has emerged as the major focus of 6G wireless research. FR3 cellular operation presents the challenge of extreme bandwidth combined with physically large antenna arrays. In this regime, conventional phase-shift beamforming entails a loss of coherence (beam-squint), and has to be replaced by true time delay beamforming (TTD). It happens that TTD is mathematically equivalent to taking the Radon transform of the space/time measurements. We exploit fifty years of research in the application of the Radon transform to computer tomography and to seismic exploration to elucidate the workings of TTD. We use the Radon transform combined with semblance detection and Radon slowness filtering to remove far-field signals from the measured space/time signals from a linear array, leaving only near-field signals. In turn we partition the array into sub-arrays. For each sub-array we estimate, via the semblance Radon transform, the angles-of-arrival of the near-field signals. We then use triangulation to estimate the coordinates of the near-field sources. Finally we integrate the original space/time data along hyperbolic trajectories to extract the individual near-field signal envelopes.

AI.tldr: This paper applies Radon transform-based methods (from CT and seismic research) to enable true time delay beamforming for 6G FR3 band, using semblance detection and slowness filtering to separate near-field from far-field signals, then estimating near-field source coordinates via sub-array triangulation.
AI.motivation: FR3 band for 6G faces extreme bandwidth and large antenna arrays where conventional phase-shift beamforming suffers from beam-squint, requiring true time delay (TTD) beamforming instead.
AI.method: Radon transform combined with semblance detection and slowness filtering removes far-field signals; sub-array partitioning with semblance Radon estimates near-field AoAs; triangulation yields source coordinates; hyperbolic integration extracts signal envelopes.
AI.result: Establishes mathematical equivalence between TTD beamforming and Radon transform; demonstrates near-field signal separation and localization via sub-array processing.
AI.conclusion: Fifty years of Radon transform research from medical and geophysical imaging can be directly leveraged for TTD beamforming in 6G FR3 systems.

title: EDU-Net: Retinal Pathological Fluid Segmentation in OCT Images with Multiscale Feature Fusion and Boundary Optimization
summary: Objective: Diabetic macular edema (DME) is the leading cause of severe visual impairment in patients with diabetes. Quantification of retinal fluid, particularly intraretinal fluid (IRF) and subretinal fluid (SRF), plays a critical role in the management of DME. Although optical coherence tomography (OCT) can be used for detection, the variable morphology of fluid accumulation and the blurred boundaries caused by noise interference still limit the accuracy of OCT's automatic segmentation. Methods: Retrospective model development and validation study. This study proposes a novel edge-guided dual-branch encoder-decoder network (EDU-Net) to achieve accurate and efficient automatic segmentation of OCT liquid lesions. The local feature extraction branch is based on the EfficientNet model, which precisely captures tiny lesions by leveraging its lightweight separable convolution and high-resolution feature preservation strategy. The global feature extraction branch is based on the large-kernel efficient convolution (LKEC) module and the downsampling layer design to enhance long-range dependencies and global semantics. EDU-Net applies a multi-category edge-guided attention module to fuse high-frequency boundary detail information to each resolution feature to optimize the boundary segmentation performance. Results: Extensive results on the in-house and public datasets demonstrate that EDU-Net achieves state-of-the-art DSC segmentation performance in terms of efficiency and robustness, especially in the segmentation of IRF lesions. Conclusions: EDU-Net integrates local details with global context and optimizes boundaries, achieving an improvement in the accuracy of automatic segmentation of retinal fluid.

AI.tldr: This paper presents EDU-Net, an edge-guided dual-branch encoder-decoder network for automatic segmentation of retinal fluid (IRF and SRF) in OCT images for diabetic macular edema management, achieving state-of-the-art performance by combining local detail capture (EfficientNet), global context (large-kernel convolutions), and edge-guided attention for boundary optimization.
AI.motivation: DME is a leading cause of vision loss in diabetic patients, and accurate quantification of retinal fluid (IRF/SRF) is critical but challenged by variable fluid morphology and noise-blurred boundaries in OCT imaging.
AI.method: EDU-Net is a dual-branch encoder-decoder network: (1) a local feature extraction branch using EfficientNet for lightweight separable convolution and high-resolution feature preservation to capture tiny lesions; (2) a global feature extraction branch using large-kernel efficient convolution (LKEC) modules and downsampling to enhance long-range dependencies; plus a multi-category edge-guided attention module to fuse boundary details across resolutions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on both in-house and public datasets show EDU-Net achieves state-of-the-art DSC segmentation performance, particularly excelling in IRF lesion segmentation with strong efficiency and robustness.
AI.conclusion: EDU-Net effectively integrates local details with global context and optimizes boundary segmentation, leading to improved accuracy in automatic retinal fluid segmentation for DME management.

title: Watts-per-Intelligence Part II: Algorithmic Catalysis
summary: We develop a thermodynamic theory of algorithmic catalysis within the watts-per-intelligence framework, identifying reusable computational structures that reduce irreversible operations for a task class while satisfying bounded restoration and structural selectivity constraints. We prove that any class-specific speed-up is upper-bounded by the algorithmic mutual information between the substrate and the class descriptor, and that installing this information incurs a minimum thermodynamic cost via Landauer erasure. Combining these results yields a coupling theorem that lower-bounds the deployment horizon required for a catalyst to be energetically favourable. The framework is illustrated on an affine SAT class and situates contemporary learned systems within a unified information-thermodynamic constraint on intelligent computation.

AI.tldr: Develops a thermodynamic theory of algorithmic catalysis, proving that computational speed-ups are bounded by algorithmic mutual information, and deriving the minimum thermodynamic cost and deployment horizon for catalysts to be energetically favorable.
AI.motivation: To establish a unified information-thermodynamic constraint on intelligent computation by understanding how reusable computational structures can reduce irreversible operations across task classes.
AI.method: Builds a thermodynamic theory within the watts-per-intelligence framework, using algorithmic mutual information and Landauer's principle to derive bounds on speed-up and thermodynamic costs of installing catalytic information.
AI.result: Class-specific speed-up is upper-bounded by algorithmic mutual information between substrate and class descriptor; installing this information incurs minimum thermodynamic cost via Landauer erasure; a coupling theorem lower-bounds the deployment horizon for energetic favorability.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unified constraint on intelligent computation, illustrated on an affine SAT class, and situates contemporary learned systems within these information-thermodynamic limits.

title: Thinking Like a Botanist: Challenging Multimodal Language Models with Intent-Driven Chain-of-Inquiry
summary: Vision evaluations are typically done through multi-step processes. In most contemporary fields, experts analyze images using structured, evidence-based adaptive questioning. In plant pathology, botanists inspect leaf images, identify visual cues, infer diagnostic intent, and probe further with targeted questions that adapt to species, symptoms, and severity. This structured probing is crucial for accurate disease diagnosis and treatment formulation. Yet current vision-language models are evaluated on single-turn question answering. To address this gap, we introduce PlantInquiryVQA, a benchmark for studying multi-step, intent-driven visual reasoning in botanical diagnosis. We formalize a Chain of Inquiry framework modeling diagnostic trajectories as ordered question-answer sequences conditioned on grounded visual cues and explicit epistemic intent. We release a dataset of 24,950 expert-curated plant images and 138,068 question-answer pairs annotated with visual grounding, severity labels, and domain-specific reasoning templates. Evaluations on top-tier Multimodal Large Language Models reveal that while they describe visual symptoms adequately, they struggle with safe clinical reasoning and accurate diagnosis. Importantly, structured question-guided inquiry significantly improves diagnostic correctness, reduces hallucination, and increases reasoning efficiency. We hope PlantInquiryVQA serves as a foundational benchmark in advancing research to train diagnostic agents to reason like expert botanists rather than static classifiers.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PlantInquiryVQA, a benchmark for evaluating multi-step, intent-driven visual reasoning in plant disease diagnosis. It reveals that current MLLMs struggle with clinical reasoning but improve significantly when guided by structured questioning.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models are evaluated on single-turn QA, but real-world plant pathology diagnosis involves multi-step adaptive questioning. There is no benchmark to evaluate this structured probing capability in MLLMs.
AI.method: The authors formalize a Chain of Inquiry framework with diagnostic trajectories as ordered QA sequences conditioned on visual cues and epistemic intent. They release a dataset of 24,950 images and 138,068 QA pairs with visual grounding, severity labels, and reasoning templates.
AI.result: MLLMs describe visual symptoms adequately but struggle with safe clinical reasoning and accurate diagnosis. Structured question-guided inquiry improves diagnostic correctness, reduces hallucination, and increases reasoning efficiency.
AI.conclusion: PlantInquiryVQA serves as a foundational benchmark for training diagnostic agents to reason like expert botanists rather than static classifiers.

title: Frequency-Forcing: From Scaling-as-Time to Soft Frequency Guidance
summary: While standard flow-matching models transport noise to data uniformly, incorporating an explicit generation order - specifically, establishing coarse, low-frequency structure before fine detail - has proven highly effective for synthesizing natural images. Two recent works offer distinct paradigms for this. K-Flow imposes a hard frequency constraint by reinterpreting a frequency scaling variable as flow time, running the trajectory inside a transformed amplitude space. Latent Forcing provides a soft ordering mechanism by coupling the pixel flow with an auxiliary semantic latent flow via asynchronous time schedules, leaving the pixel interpolation path itself untouched. Viewed from the angle of improving pixel generation, we observe that forcing - guiding generation with an earlier-maturing auxiliary stream - offers a highly compatible route to scale-ordered generation without rewriting the core flow coordinate. Building on this, we propose Frequency-Forcing, which realizes K-Flow's frequency ordering through Latent Forcing's soft mechanism: a standard pixel flow is guided by an auxiliary low-frequency stream that matures earlier in time. Unlike Latent Forcing, whose scratchpad relies on a heavy pretrained encoder (e.g., DINO), our frequency scratchpad is derived from the data itself via a lightweight learnable wavelet packet transform. We term this a self-forcing signal, which avoids external dependencies while learning a basis better adapted to data statistics than the fixed bases used in hard frequency flows. On ImageNet-256, Frequency-Forcing consistently improves FID over strong pixel- and latent-space baselines, and naturally composes with a semantic stream to yield further gains. This illustrates that forcing-based scale ordering is a versatile, path-preserving alternative to hard frequency flows.

AI.tldr: Frequency-Forcing improves image generation by using a lightweight, learnable wavelet-based low-frequency stream to guide a standard pixel flow, achieving scale-ordered generation without modifying the core flow path.
AI.motivation: Incorporating generation order (coarse-to-fine) improves image synthesis. Two prior works (K-Flow with hard frequency constraints, Latent Forcing with soft semantic guidance) offer different paradigms. The authors observe that forcing (soft guidance from an auxiliary stream) is a more compatible route for scale-ordered generation without rewriting the core flow.
AI.method: Frequency-Forcing guides a standard pixel flow using an auxiliary low-frequency stream that matures earlier in time. The low-frequency scratchpad is derived via a lightweight learnable wavelet packet transform from the data itself (self-forcing), avoiding external pretrained encoders like DINO.
AI.result: On ImageNet-256, Frequency-Forcing consistently improves FID over strong pixel- and latent-space baselines, and naturally composes with a semantic stream for further gains.
AI.conclusion: Forcing-based scale ordering is a versatile, path-preserving alternative to hard frequency flows, offering improved performance without external dependencies.

title: Architecture of an AI-Based Automated Course of Action Generation System for Military Operations
summary: The automation system for Course of Action (CoA) planning is an essential element in future warfare. As maneuver speeds increase, surveillance ranges extend, and weapon ranges grow, the operational area expands, making traditional manned-based CoA planning increasingly challenging. Consequently, the development of an AI-based automated CoA planning system is becoming increasingly necessary. Accordingly, several countries and defense organizations are actively developing AI-based CoA planning systems. However, due to security restrictions and limited public disclosure, the technical maturity of such systems remains difficult to assess. Furthermore, as these systems are military-related, their details are not publicly disclosed, making it difficult to accurately assess the current level of development. In response to this, this study aims to introduce relevant doctrines within the scope of publicly available information and present applicable AI technologies for each stage of the CoA planning process. Ultimately, it proposes an architecture for the development of an automated CoA planning system.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the growing need for AI-based automated Course of Action (CoA) planning systems in modern warfare, reviews publicly available doctrines, proposes applicable AI technologies for each stage of CoA planning, and suggests an architecture for developing such automated systems.
AI.motivation: As warfare evolves with faster maneuvers, longer surveillance, and greater weapon ranges, traditional human-based CoA planning becomes increasingly difficult, necessitating AI-based automation.
AI.method: Introduces relevant military doctrines from publicly available information and presents suitable AI technologies corresponding to each stage of the CoA planning process.
AI.result: Proposes an architecture for developing an automated CoA planning system based on the reviewed doctrines and applicable AI technologies.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a foundational framework for building AI-automated CoA planning systems, addressing a critical gap in publicly assessable technical information in this military domain.

title: Open-H-Embodiment: A Large-Scale Dataset for Enabling Foundation Models in Medical Robotics
summary: Autonomous medical robots hold promise to improve patient outcomes, reduce provider workload, democratize access to care, and enable superhuman precision. However, autonomous medical robotics has been limited by a fundamental data problem: existing medical robotic datasets are small, single-embodiment, and rarely shared openly, restricting the development of foundation models that the field needs to advance. We introduce Open-H-Embodiment, the largest open dataset of medical robotic video with synchronized kinematics to date, spanning more than 49 institutions and multiple robotic platforms including the CMR Versius, Intuitive Surgical's da Vinci, da Vinci Research Kit (dVRK), Rob Surgical BiTrack, Virtual Incision's MIRA, Moon Surgical Maestro, and a variety of custom systems, spanning surgical manipulation, robotic ultrasound, and endoscopy procedures. We demonstrate the research enabled by this dataset through two foundation models. GR00T-H is the first open foundation vision-language-action model for medical robotics, which is the only evaluated model to achieve full end-to-end task completion on a structured suturing benchmark (25% of trials vs. 0% for all others) and achieves 64% average success across a 29-step ex vivo suturing sequence. We also train Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator, the first action-conditioned world model to enable multi-embodiment surgical simulation from a single checkpoint, spanning nine robotic platforms and supporting in silico policy evaluation and synthetic data generation for the medical domain. These results suggest that open, large-scale medical robot data collection can serve as critical infrastructure for the research community, enabling advances in robot learning, world modeling, and beyond.

AI.tldr: Open-H-Embodiment is the largest open dataset of medical robotic video with synchronized kinematics, collected from 49+ institutions and multiple robotic platforms. The paper presents two foundation models: GR00T-H (first open VLA model for medical robotics, achieving 25% full task completion vs 0% for others) and Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator (first action-conditioned world model supporting multi-embodiment surgical simulation).
AI.motivation: Autonomous medical robotics has been limited by a fundamental data problem: existing medical robotic datasets are small, single-embodiment, and rarely shared openly, restricting the development of foundation models needed to advance the field.
AI.method: The authors collected the largest open dataset of medical robotic video with synchronized kinematics from 49+ institutions and multiple robotic platforms (CMR Versius, da Vinci, dVRK, Rob Surgical BiTrack, MIRA, Moon Surgical Maestro, custom systems). They trained two foundation models: GR00T-H (vision-language-action model) and Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator (action-conditioned world model).
AI.result: GR00T-H is the only evaluated model to achieve full end-to-end task completion on a structured suturing benchmark (25% of trials vs 0% for all others) and achieves 64% average success across a 29-step ex vivo suturing sequence. Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator supports multi-embodiment surgical simulation from a single checkpoint across nine robotic platforms.
AI.conclusion: Open, large-scale medical robot data collection can serve as critical infrastructure for the research community, enabling advances in robot learning, world modeling, and beyond.

title: Complex Approximate Message Passing with Non-separable Denoising
summary: Approximate Message Passing (AMP) is a general framework for iterative algorithms, originally developed for compressed sensing and later extended to a wide range of high-dimensional inference problems. Although recent work has advanced matrix AMP, complex AMP, and AMP for non-separable functions independently, a unified state evolution theory for complex AMP with non-separable denoisers has been lacking. This article fills that gap by establishing state evolution in the setting of complex, non-separable denoising functions. The proposed approach constructs an augmented real-valued system that lifts the problem to a higher-dimensional space, then recovers the complex domain through a many-to-one canonical transformation. Under this construction, the Onsager correction naturally involves Wirtinger derivatives, and the resulting state evolution reduces to scalar complex recursions despite the non-separable structure of the denoisers. The framework extends to the matrix-valued setting, accommodating multiple feature vectors simultaneously. This generalization enables AMP to exploit joint structural constraints, such as simultaneous group and element sparsity, in complex-valued recovery problems. The complex sparse group least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) serves as a key instantiation, motivated by preamble detection in Orthogonal Time-Frequency Space (OTFS)-based unsourced random access. Numerical experiments confirm that state evolution accurately predicts performance and show that complex non-separable denoising can produce significant gains over separable and real-valued alternatives.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a unified state evolution theory for complex Approximate Message Passing (AMP) with non-separable denoisers, using an augmented real-valued system and Wirtinger derivatives, extending to matrix-valued settings and demonstrating gains in complex sparse group LASSO for OTFS-based unsourced random access.
AI.motivation: Previous work advanced matrix AMP, complex AMP, and AMP for non-separable functions independently, but a unified state evolution theory for complex AMP with non-separable denoisers was lacking.
AI.method: The authors construct an augmented real-valued system to lift the problem to higher-dimensional space, then recover the complex domain via a many-to-one canonical transformation. The Onsager correction naturally involves Wirtinger derivatives, and state evolution reduces to scalar complex recursions despite non-separable denoisers. The framework extends to matrix-valued settings for multiple feature vectors.
AI.result: State evolution accurately predicts performance; complex non-separable denoising produces significant gains over separable and real-valued alternatives, demonstrated on complex sparse group LASSO for OTFS-based unsourced random access preamble detection.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified framework fills the theoretical gap for complex AMP with non-separable denoisers, enabling exploitation of joint structural constraints like simultaneous group and element sparsity in complex-valued recovery problems.

title: DiffNR: Diffusion-Enhanced Neural Representation Optimization for Sparse-View 3D Tomographic Reconstruction
comment: Accepted to AAAI 2026. Project page: https://ooonesevennn.github.io/DiffNR/
summary: Neural representations (NRs), such as neural fields and 3D Gaussians, effectively model volumetric data in computed tomography (CT) but suffer from severe artifacts under sparse-view settings. To address this, we propose DiffNR, a novel framework that enhances NR optimization with diffusion priors. At its core is SliceFixer, a single-step diffusion model designed to correct artifacts in degraded slices. We integrate specialized conditioning layers into the network and develop tailored data curation strategies to support model finetuning. During reconstruction, SliceFixer periodically generates pseudo-reference volumes, providing auxiliary 3D perceptual supervision to fix underconstrained regions. Compared to prior methods that embed CT solvers into time-consuming iterative denoising, our repair-and-augment strategy avoids frequent diffusion model queries, leading to better runtime performance. Extensive experiments show that DiffNR improves PSNR by 3.99 dB on average, generalizes well across domains, and maintains efficient optimization.

AI.tldr: DiffNR enhances neural representations (NRs) for sparse-view CT reconstruction by integrating a single-step diffusion model (SliceFixer) that corrects artifacts in degraded slices and provides auxiliary 3D supervision, achieving significant PSNR gains (3.99 dB) with efficient runtime.
AI.motivation: Neural representations suffer from severe artifacts under sparse-view CT settings, and existing diffusion-based methods require time-consuming iterative denoising.
AI.method: Proposes DiffNR with SliceFixer, a single-step diffusion model that corrects artifacts in degraded slices. Uses specialized conditioning layers and tailored data curation for finetuning. SliceFixer periodically generates pseudo-reference volumes to provide auxiliary 3D perceptual supervision for underconstrained regions.
AI.result: Improves PSNR by 3.99 dB on average, generalizes well across domains, and maintains efficient optimization.
AI.conclusion: The repair-and-augment strategy avoids frequent diffusion model queries, achieving better runtime performance than prior iterative denoising methods.

title: DiP-SD: Distributed Pipelined Speculative Decoding for Efficient LLM Inference at the Edge
comment: Accepted by 2026 IEEE 103rd Vehicular Technology Conference (VTC2026-Spring)
summary: Speculative decoding has emerged as a promising technique for large language model (LLM) inference by accelerating autoregressive decoding via draft-then-verify. This paper studies a new edge scenario with multi-user inference, where draft tokens are generated locally on devices and subsequently offloaded to a centralized edge server for batch verification. The key challenge is to sustain high throughput under coupled decisions of (i) batching and pipeline scheduling and (ii) per user draft token length. We propose DiP-SD, which exploits two complementary parallelism dimensions: device-level distributed drafting and phase-level draft-verify pipelining. We formulate a throughput-maximization objective, defined as the expected number of accepted tokens per unit time, and jointly optimize the number of batches, user-to-batch assignment, and integer draft lengths. To solve the resulting fractional mixed-integer program, DiP-SD scans the batch number and iteratively alternates between an association subproblem and a draft-length subproblem. Numerical results under a Qwen3-1.7B/Qwen3-32B device-edge deployment show that DiP-SD achieves up to 17.89x throughput over autoregressive decoding (AD) and 1.93x over AD with greedy batching.

AI.tldr: DiP-SD accelerates multi-user LLM inference by jointly optimizing batching, scheduling, and draft token lengths in speculative decoding, achieving up to 17.89x throughput over autoregressive decoding.
AI.motivation: To address the throughput bottleneck in multi-user edge inference scenarios where draft tokens are generated locally and verified on a centralized server, requiring coordinated optimization of batching/pipeline scheduling and per-user draft lengths.
AI.method: Proposes DiP-SD, which exploits device-level distributed drafting and phase-level draft-verify pipelining. Formulates a throughput-maximization objective and solves a fractional mixed-integer program by scanning batch numbers and iteratively alternating between association and draft-length subproblems.
AI.result: DiP-SD achieves up to 17.89x throughput over autoregressive decoding and 1.93x over autoregressive decoding with greedy batching in a Qwen3-1.7B/Qwen3-32B device-edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of batching, user-to-batch assignment, and draft lengths significantly improves throughput for multi-user speculative decoding in edge scenarios.

title: Linear Image Generation by Synthesizing Exposure Brackets
summary: The life of a photo begins with photons striking the sensor, whose signals are passed through a sophisticated image signal processing (ISP) pipeline to produce a display-referred image. However, such images are no longer faithful to the incident light, being compressed in dynamic range and stylized by subjective preferences. In contrast, RAW images record direct sensor signals before non-linear tone mapping. After camera response curve correction and demosaicing, they can be converted into linear images, which are scene-referred representations that directly reflect true irradiance and are invariant to sensor-specific factors. Since image sensors have better dynamic range and bit depth, linear images contain richer information than display-referred ones, leaving users more room for editing during post-processing. Despite this advantage, current generative models mainly synthesize display-referred images, which inherently limits downstream editing. In this paper, we address the task of text-to-linear-image generation: synthesizing a high-quality, scene-referred linear image that preserves full dynamic range, conditioned on a text prompt, for professional post-processing. Generating linear images is challenging, as pre-trained VAEs in latent diffusion models struggle to simultaneously preserve extreme highlights and shadows due to the higher dynamic range and bit depth. To this end, we represent a linear image as a sequence of exposure brackets, each capturing a specific portion of the dynamic range, and propose a DiT-based flow-matching architecture for text-conditioned exposure bracket generation. We further demonstrate downstream applications including text-guided linear image editing and structure-conditioned generation via ControlNet.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces text-to-linear-image generation, using exposure brackets and a DiT-based flow-matching architecture to produce scene-referred linear images with full dynamic range for professional post-processing.
AI.motivation: Current generative models mostly synthesize display-referred (compressed, tone-mapped) images, limiting downstream editing. The authors argue that linear images (scene-referred) retain true irradiance and offer richer editing potential, motivating the need for text-to-linear-image generation.
AI.method: Linear images are represented as a sequence of exposure brackets, each covering a portion of the dynamic range. A DiT-based (Diffusion Transformer) flow-matching architecture is proposed for text-conditioned exposure bracket generation.
AI.result: The method enables text-to-linear-image synthesis that preserves full dynamic range. Downstream applications include text-guided linear image editing and structure-conditioned generation via ControlNet.
AI.conclusion: By generating linear images directly, the work enables richer post-processing flexibility, overcoming limitations of pre-trained VAEs in handling high dynamic range and bit depth.

title: Reinforcing privacy reasoning in LLMs via normative simulacra from fiction
summary: Information handling practices of LLM agents are broadly misaligned with the contextual privacy expectations of their users. Contextual Integrity (CI) provides a principled framework, defining privacy as the appropriate flow of information within context-relative norms. However, existing approaches either double inference cost via supervisor-assistant architectures, or fine-tune on narrow task-specific data. We propose extracting normative simulacra (structured representations of norms and information flows) from fiction novels and using them to fine-tune LLMs via supervised learning followed by GRPO reinforcement learning. Our composite reward function combines programmatic signals, including task clarity (subsuming schema validity, construct discrimination, and extraction confidence), structural completeness, internal consistency, and context identification, with an LLM judge that evaluates whether the model's privacy reasoning is grounded in the held-out normative universe of the source text. To mitigate overfitting, we introduce per-completion contrastive scoring: each completion is evaluated against both the correct normative universe and a randomly selected wrong one, teaching the model to condition on context rather than memorize source-specific norms. We evaluate on five CI-aligned benchmarks spanning distinct societal contexts and ablate the contributions of RL and normative grounding. Across seven models, SFT introduces a conservative prior toward restricting information flow, improving recognition of privacy-relevant situations but not the correctness of privacy judgments. GRPO with normative grounding achieves the highest score on a law compliance benchmark and strongest correlation with crowdsourced human privacy expectations, demonstrating that fiction-derived normative simulacra can teach contextual privacy reasoning that transfers to real-world domains.

AI.tldr: LLM agents misalign with user privacy expectations. This paper uses fictional novels to extract normative simulacra (structured privacy norms), fine-tunes LLMs via SFT + GRPO reinforcement learning with a composite reward function and per-completion contrastive scoring, and shows that fiction-derived norms transfer to real-world privacy reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents fail to respect contextual privacy expectations. Existing CI-based solutions are either too costly (dual-model architectures) or too narrow (task-specific fine-tuning). The authors aim to teach generalizable contextual privacy reasoning without these limitations.
AI.method: Extract normative simulacra from fiction novels → Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) → GRPO reinforcement learning. Reward function includes programmatic signals (task clarity, structural completeness, etc.) plus an LLM judge. Per-completion contrastive scoring prevents overfitting by comparing against correct vs. incorrect normative universes.
AI.result: SFT alone improves privacy situation recognition but not correctness. GRPO with normative grounding achieves best performance on a law compliance benchmark and strongest correlation with human privacy expectations across seven models tested.
AI.conclusion: Fiction-derived normative simulacra can effectively teach LLMs contextual privacy reasoning that transfers to real-world domains, validated by alignment with human privacy expectations.

title: Escaping the Agreement Trap: Defensibility Signals for Evaluating Rule-Governed AI
comment: 22 pages, 10 figures, preprint. Research on Defensibility Index (DI), Ambiguity Index (AI), and Probabilistic Defensibility Signal (PDS) for policy-grounded evaluation of rule-governed AI in content moderation (Reddit production data)
summary: Content moderation systems are typically evaluated by measuring agreement with human labels. In rule-governed environments this assumption fails: multiple decisions may be logically consistent with the governing policy, and agreement metrics penalize valid decisions while mischaracterizing ambiguity as error - a failure mode we term the Agreement Trap. We formalize evaluation as policy-grounded correctness and introduce the Defensibility Index (DI) and Ambiguity Index (AI). To estimate reasoning stability without additional audit passes, we introduce the Probabilistic Defensibility Signal (PDS), derived from audit-model token logprobs. We harness LLM reasoning traces as a governance signal rather than a classification output by deploying the audit model not to decide whether content violates policy, but to verify whether a proposed decision is logically derivable from the governing rule hierarchy. We validate the framework on 193,000+ Reddit moderation decisions across multiple communities and evaluation cohorts, finding a 33-46.6 percentage-point gap between agreement-based and policy-grounded metrics, with 79.8-80.6% of the model's false negatives corresponding to policy-grounded decisions rather than true errors. We further show that measured ambiguity is driven by rule specificity: auditing 37,286 identical decisions under three tiers of the same community rules reduces AI by 10.8 pp while DI remains stable. Repeated-sampling analysis attributes PDS variance primarily to governance ambiguity rather than decoding noise. A Governance Gate built on these signals achieves 78.6% automation coverage with 64.9% risk reduction. Together, these results show that evaluation in rule-governed environments should shift from agreement with historical labels to reasoning-grounded validity under explicit rules.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies the "Agreement Trap" in content moderation evaluation—where agreement metrics penalize valid decisions that are logically consistent with policy but diverge from human labels. The authors propose policy-grounded evaluation using Defensibility Index (DI), Ambiguity Index (AI), and Probabilistic Defensibility Signal (PDS), showing a 33-46.6 pp gap between agreement-based and policy-grounded metrics on 193,000+ Reddit decisions, and advocate shifting evaluation from label-matching to reasoning-validity under explicit rules.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation metrics for content moderation systems rely on agreement with human labels, but in rule-governed environments, multiple decisions can be logically consistent with policy. This mischaracterizes valid, ambiguous decisions as errors (the "Agreement Trap"), motivating a need for policy-grounded evaluation frameworks.
AI.method: The authors formalize policy-grounded correctness, introduce Defensibility Index (DI) and Ambiguity Index (AI), and propose Probabilistic Defensibility Signal (PDS) using audit-model token logprobs to estimate reasoning stability. The audit model verifies whether a decision is logically derivable from rule hierarchies rather than classifying content directly. Validated on 193,000+ Reddit moderation decisions across communities and evaluation cohorts.
AI.result: A 33-46.6 percentage-point gap between agreement-based and policy-grounded metrics; 79.8-80.6% of model false negatives are policy-grounded decisions not errors; auditing under three rule tiers reduces AI by 10.8 pp while DI stays stable; PDS variance is attributed to governance ambiguity over decoding noise; Governance Gate achieves 78.6% automation coverage with 64.9% risk reduction.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation in rule-governed environments should shift from agreement with historical labels to reasoning-grounded validity under explicit rules, as traditional agreement metrics systematically mischaracterize ambiguity as error.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Manipulation Understanding with Enriched Semantic Event Chains
summary: Robotic systems operating in human environments must reason about how object interactions evolve over time, which actions are currently being performed, and what manipulation step is likely to follow. Classical enriched Semantic Event Chains (eSECs) provide an interpretable relational description of manipulation, but remain primarily descriptive and do not directly support uncertainty-aware decision making. In this paper, we propose eSEC-LAM, a neuro-symbolic framework that transforms eSECs into an explicit event-level symbolic state for manipulation understanding. The proposed formulation augments classical eSECs with confidence-aware predicates, functional object roles, affordance priors, primitive-level abstraction, and saliency-guided explanation cues. These enriched symbolic states are derived from a foundation-model-based perception front-end through deterministic predicate extraction, while current-action inference and next-primitive prediction are performed using lightweight symbolic reasoning over primitive pre- and post-conditions. We evaluate the proposed framework on EPIC-KITCHENS-100, EPIC-KITCHENS VISOR, and Assembly101 across action recognition, next-primitive prediction, robustness to perception noise, and explanation consistency. Experimental results show that eSEC-LAM achieves competitive action recognition, substantially improves next-primitive prediction, remains more robust under degraded perceptual conditions than both classical symbolic and end-to-end video baselines, and provides temporally consistent explanation traces grounded in explicit relational evidence. These findings demonstrate that enriched Semantic Event Chains can serve not only as interpretable descriptors of manipulation, but also as effective internal states for neuro-symbolic action reasoning.

AI.tldr: eSEC-LAM is a neuro-symbolic framework that enhances classical Semantic Event Chains with confidence-aware predicates and affordance priors, enabling interpretable manipulation understanding, action recognition, and next-primitive prediction that remains robust under perceptual noise.
AI.motivation: Robotic systems need to reason about object interactions over time and predict manipulation steps, but classical eSECs are purely descriptive and cannot handle uncertainty-aware decision making.
AI.method: Proposes eSEC-LAM, a neuro-symbolic framework that augments eSECs with confidence-aware predicates, functional object roles, affordance priors, primitive-level abstraction, and saliency cues. Uses foundation-model-based perception for deterministic predicate extraction, then lightweight symbolic reasoning over pre/post-conditions for action inference and next-primitive prediction.
AI.result: Evaluated on EPIC-KITCHENS-100, EPIC-KITCHENS VISOR, and Assembly101. Achieves competitive action recognition, substantially improved next-primitive prediction, greater robustness under degraded perception compared to symbolic and end-to-end baselines, and provides temporally consistent explanation traces.
AI.conclusion: Enriched Semantic Event Chains can serve not only as interpretable descriptors but also as effective internal states for neuro-symbolic action reasoning in robotic manipulation.

title: Threat Detection and Resilience Techniques in PRS-Assisted OTDOA 5G Positioning Systems
comment: 10 pages, 9 figures, Accepted for publication for NAVITEC 2026
summary: Precise positioning is a key enabler for emerging 5G applications, from autonomous transport to industrial automation. Yet the open physical layer (PL) leaves standard positioning reference signals (PRSs) vulnerable to manipulation. This work addresses the security of downlink observed time difference of arrival positioning (DL-OTDOA) through three contributions. First, we introduce VeriLoc, an open-source system-level simulator designed for realistic channel modeling and PL threat injection. Second, we propose three novel security techniques to enhance resilience and threat detection: encrypted PRS to prevent adversarial waveform synthesis, angular-based source authentication (ABSA), and a cross-layer downlink-uplink handshaking protocol to detect attacks that cannot be mitigated by encryption. Third, utilizing VeriLoc, we evaluate the proposed techniques alongside position tracking and a PRS authentication scheme, which extends the original hash-based message authentication code (HMAC) scheme design to support digital signatures. Simulation results demonstrate that while encryption, authentication schemes, and tracking robustly counter selective PRS spoofing and jamming, the proposed spatial and cross-layer mechanisms are essential for detecting meaconing, collectively maintaining attack detection rates in excess of 90% while keeping false alarm rates minimal.

AI.tldr: VeriLoc is an open-source simulator for 5G DL-OTDOA positioning security analysis. Three security techniques (encrypted PRS, angular-based source authentication, cross-layer handshaking) are proposed and evaluated, achieving >90% attack detection with minimal false alarms against spoofing, jamming, and meaconing threats.
AI.motivation: The open physical layer of 5G leaves standard positioning reference signals (PRS) vulnerable to manipulation (spoofing, jamming, meaconing), which threatens positioning-dependent applications like autonomous transport and industrial automation.
AI.method: (1) Built VeriLoc, an open-source system-level simulator for realistic channel modeling and PL threat injection; (2) Proposed three security techniques: encrypted PRS, angular-based source authentication (ABSA), and cross-layer DL-UL handshaking protocol; (3) Extended existing HMAC scheme to support digital signatures.
AI.result: Encryption, authentication, and tracking robustly counter PRS spoofing and jamming. Spatial and cross-layer mechanisms are essential for detecting meaconing. Combined techniques achieve >90% attack detection rates with minimal false alarms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-layered security approach (encryption + spatial authentication + cross-layer handshaking) effectively secures 5G DL-OTDOA positioning, and VeriLoc serves as a valuable open-source tool for future research in this domain.

title: Downlink Channel Matrix Estimation from PMI-Only Feedback in FDD Systems: Maximum Likelihood and Sharp Excess Risk Bound
summary:
We study downlink channel estimation in a frequency-division duplex (FDD) massive MIMO system from PMI-only feedback under a 5G NR-type limited-feedback architecture.
  In this architecture, the user selects a preferred codeword from a shared codebook based on the reduced-dimensional channel and only reports its index (known as the precoding matrix indicator, PMI) back to the base station. Therefore, the channel must be estimated from these highly quantized, nonlinear PMI observations. Based on a probabilistic perturbation model, a constrained maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) is proposed for this estimation problem, whose objective can also be interpreted as a relaxation of the hard empirical decision error. The Cramér--Rao bound is derived for the complex-valued model, with the global phase ambiguity handled via gauge-fixing. For the real-valued setting, a global excess-risk bound of order $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ is established, which is then refined to a sharp local rate of order $O(1/T)$ under suitable identifiability conditions. Numerical results show that the MLE asymptotically attains the Cramér--Rao bound and outperforms several baseline methods on both synthetic data and realistic FDD channels.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a constrained maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) for downlink channel estimation in FDD massive MIMO systems using only highly quantized PMI feedback, demonstrating asymptotic optimality and outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: In FDD massive MIMO systems with limited feedback (5G NR-type), the downlink channel must be estimated from only PMI (precoding matrix indicator) reports—highly quantized, nonlinear observations. Traditional channel estimation methods cannot handle this extreme loss of information, motivating a new estimation framework.
AI.method: A constrained maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) is developed based on a probabilistic perturbation model. The Cramér-Rao bound is derived with gauge-fixing for global phase ambiguity. Theoretical analysis provides global excess-risk bound O(1/√T) and sharp local rate O(1/T) under identifiability conditions.
AI.result: The MLE asymptotically attains the Cramér-Rao bound. Numerical experiments show it outperforms several baseline methods on both synthetic data and realistic FDD channels.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PMI-based channel estimation approach is theoretically grounded and practically effective, achieving near-optimal performance despite extreme quantization in 5G NR feedback architectures.

title: Micro-DualNet: Dual-Path Spatio-Temporal Network for Micro-Action Recognition
comment: Accepted to International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG)
summary: Micro-actions are subtle, localized movements lasting 1-3 seconds such as scratching one's head or tapping fingers. Such subtle actions are essential for social communication, ubiquitously used in natural interactions, and thus critical for fine-grained video understanding, yet remain poorly understood by current computer vision systems. We identify a fundamental challenge: micro-actions exhibit diverse spatio-temporal characteristics where some are defined by spatial configurations while others manifest through temporal dynamics. Existing methods that commit to a single spatio-temporal decomposition cannot accommodate this diversity. We propose a dual-path network that processes anatomically-grounded spatial entities through parallel Spatial-Temporal (ST) and Temporal-Spatial (TS) pathways. The ST path captures spatial configurations before modeling temporal dynamics, while the TS path inverts this order to prioritize temporal dynamics. Rather than fixed fusion, we introduce entity-level adaptive routing where each body part learns its optimal processing preference, complemented by Mutual Action Consistency (MAC) loss that enforces cross-path coherence. Extensive experiments demonstrate competitive performance on MA-52 dataset and state-of-the-art results on iMiGUE dataset. Our work reveals that architectural adaptation to the inherent complexity of micro-actions is essential for advancing fine-grained video understanding.

AI.tldr: Proposes a dual-path network with adaptive routing to handle micro-actions' diverse spatio-temporal characteristics, achieving SOTA on iMiGUE and competitive results on MA-52.
AI.motivation: Micro-actions exhibit diverse spatio-temporal characteristics (some spatial, some temporal), but existing methods use fixed spatio-temporal decompositions that cannot accommodate this diversity.
AI.method: A dual-path network with parallel ST (Spatial→Temporal) and TS (Temporal→Spatial) pathways, entity-level adaptive routing for body parts, and Mutual Action Consistency (MAC) loss.
AI.result: Competitive performance on MA-52 dataset and state-of-the-art results on iMiGUE dataset.
AI.conclusion: Architectural adaptation to micro-actions' inherent complexity is essential for advancing fine-grained video understanding.

title: Do Masked Autoencoders Improve Downhole Prediction? An Empirical Study on Real Well Drilling Data
summary: Downhole drilling telemetry presents a fundamental labeling asymmetry: surface sensor data are generated continuously at 1~Hz, while labeled downhole measurements are costly, intermittent, and scarce. Current machine learning approaches for downhole metric prediction universally adopt fully supervised training from scratch, which is poorly suited to this data regime. We present the first empirical evaluation of masked autoencoder (MAE) pretraining for downhole drilling metric prediction. Using two publicly available Utah FORGE geothermal wells comprising approximately 3.5 million timesteps of multivariate drilling telemetry, we conduct a systematic full-factorial design space search across 72 MAE configurations and compare them against supervised LSTM and GRU baselines on the task of predicting Total Mud Volume. Results show that the best MAE configuration reduces test mean absolute error by 19.8\% relative to the supervised GRU baseline, while trailing the supervised LSTM baseline by 6.4\%. Analysis of design dimensions reveals that latent space width is the dominant architectural choice (Pearson $r = -0.59$ with test MAE), while masking ratio has negligible effect, an unexpected finding attributed to high temporal redundancy in 1~Hz drilling data. These results establish MAE pretraining as a viable paradigm for drilling analytics and identify the conditions under which it is most beneficial.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates masked autoencoder (MAE) pretraining for downhole drilling metric prediction, finding it reduces error by 19.8% compared to supervised GRU but trails LSTM by 6.4%, with latent space width being the key design factor.
AI.motivation: Downhole drilling telemetry has a labeling asymmetry where surface data is abundant but labeled downhole measurements are scarce. Current supervised learning from scratch is ill-suited for this data regime, motivating the need for self-supervised pretraining approaches like MAE.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic full-factorial design space search across 72 MAE configurations and compare against supervised LSTM and GRU baselines on predicting Total Mud Volume, using two Utah FORGE geothermal wells (~3.5 million timesteps).
AI.result: Best MAE configuration reduces test MAE by 19.8% relative to supervised GRU but trails supervised LSTM by 6.4%. Latent space width is the dominant architectural factor (r=-0.59 with test MAE), while masking ratio has negligible effect due to high temporal redundancy in 1Hz drilling data.
AI.conclusion: MAE pretraining is a viable paradigm for drilling analytics, and the study identifies key conditions (latent space width matters, masking ratio does not) under which it is most beneficial.

title: Co-Evolving LLM Decision and Skill Bank Agents for Long-Horizon Tasks
summary: Long horizon interactive environments are a testbed for evaluating agents skill usage abilities. These environments demand multi step reasoning, the chaining of multiple skills over many timesteps, and robust decision making under delayed rewards and partial observability. Games are a good testbed for evaluating agent skill usage in environments. Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising alternative as game playing agents, but they often struggle with consistent long horizon decision making because they lack a mechanism to discover, retain, and reuse structured skills across episodes. We present COSPLAY, a co evolution framework in which an LLM decision agent retrieves skills from a learnable skill bank to guide action taking, while an agent managed skill pipeline discovers reusable skills from the agents unlabeled rollouts to form a skill bank. Our framework improves both the decision agent to learn better skill retrieval and action generation, while the skill bank agent continually extracts, refines, and updates skills together with their contracts. Experiments across six game environments show that COSPLAY with an 8B base model achieves over 25.1 percent average reward improvement against four frontier LLM baselines on single player game benchmarks while remaining competitive on multi player social reasoning games.

AI.tldr: COSPLAY is a co-evolution framework where an LLM agent retrieves skills from a learnable skill bank, while a separate pipeline discovers reusable skills from unlabeled rollouts, achieving 25.1% average reward improvement over frontier LLM baselines in game environments.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with consistent long horizon decision making in interactive environments because they lack mechanisms to discover, retain, and reuse structured skills across episodes.
AI.method: A co-evolution framework with two components: (1) an LLM decision agent that retrieves skills from a learnable skill bank to guide actions, and (2) a skill pipeline agent that discovers, extracts, refines, and updates reusable skills from unlabeled rollouts to populate the skill bank.
AI.result: With an 8B base model, COSPLAY achieves 25.1% average reward improvement over four frontier LLM baselines on single-player games, while remaining competitive on multi-player social reasoning games.
AI.conclusion: The co-evolution framework effectively enables continuous skill discovery and reuse, significantly improving LLM performance in long horizon interactive environments.

title: Impact-Aware Model Predictive Control for UAV Landing on a Heaving Platform
comment: To be published in the proceedings of International Federation of Automatic Control (IFAC) World Congress 2026
summary: Landing UAVs on heaving marine platforms is challenging because relative vertical motion can generate large impact forces and cause rebound on touchdown. To address this, we develop an impact-aware Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework that models landing as a velocity-level rigid-body impact governed by Newton's restitution law. We embed this as a linear complementarity problem (LCP) within the MPC dynamics to predict the discontinuous post-impact velocity and suppress rebound. In simulation, restitution-aware prediction reduces pre-impact relative velocity and improves landing robustness. Experiments on a heaving-deck testbed show an 86.2% reduction in post-impact deflection compared to a tracking MPC.

AI.tldr: An impact-aware MPC framework for landing UAVs on heaving platforms, using LCP-embedded dynamics to predict and reduce post-impact rebound, achieving 86.2% reduction in deflection experimentally.
AI.motivation: Landing UAVs on heaving marine platforms is challenging due to relative vertical motion causing large impact forces and rebound.
AI.method: Developed an MPC framework that models landing as a velocity-level rigid-body impact via Newton's restitution law, embedded as a linear complementarity problem (LCP) within the dynamics to predict discontinuous post-impact velocity and suppress rebound.
AI.result: Simulation shows reduced pre-impact relative velocity and improved robustness. Experiments on a heaving-deck testbed demonstrate an 86.2% reduction in post-impact deflection versus a tracking MPC.
AI.conclusion: Restitution-aware prediction significantly improves landing robustness and reduces rebound on moving platforms.

title: Efficient Design of Fronthaul-Constrained Uplink Reception for Cell-Free XL-MIMO
comment: accepted for publication in IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
summary: With the evolution of multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) technology toward extremely large (XL) MIMO systems comprising hundreds of, or more, antennas, this work investigates scalable and fronthaul-efficient reception design for the uplink of cell-free (CF) XL-MIMO systems. In such systems, the uplink signals transmitted by mobile user equipments (UEs) are jointly decoded at a central processing unit (CPU) connected to distributed access points (APs) via finite-capacity fronthaul links. We address the joint optimization of linear transform matrices, used by the APs to reduce the signal dimension and fronthaul load, and fronthaul compression strategies to maximize the uplink sumrate. A fractional programming (FP)-based iterative algorithm is first developed, followed by a reduced-complexity variant, termed accelerated FP (A-FP), along with its decentralized implementation whose fronthaul overhead remains independent of the number of AP antennas. Numerical results show that the proposed A-FP scheme significantly reduces computational complexity compared to FP implemented with general-purpose solvers, while substantially outperforming scalable baseline schemes that rely solely on local channel state information.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes scalable and fronthaul-efficient reception designs for cell-free XL-MIMO uplink systems, jointly optimizing AP linear transforms and fronthaul compression via fractional programming, with an accelerated variant (A-FP) that reduces complexity and enables decentralized implementation.
AI.motivation: To address the scalability and fronthaul efficiency challenges in cell-free XL-MIMO uplink systems, where hundreds or more antennas at distributed APs create excessive fronthaul load when raw signals are sent to the CPU for joint decoding.
AI.method: Joint optimization of AP-side linear transform matrices (for dimension reduction) and fronthaul compression strategies via fractional programming (FP). An accelerated FP (A-FP) variant with reduced complexity and decentralized implementation whose fronthaul overhead is independent of AP antenna count.
AI.result: The proposed A-FP scheme significantly reduces computational complexity compared to FP with general-purpose solvers, and substantially outperforms scalable baselines relying solely on local CSI.
AI.conclusion: The A-FP method achieves an effective balance between performance and complexity, making cell-free XL-MIMO uplink reception more practical for real deployments.

title: LLM-Steered Power Allocation for Parallel QPSK-AWGN Channels
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being explored as high-level decision modules in closed-loop systems, but their stochastic nature makes safe integration challenging. In this paper, we propose LLM-Steered Power Allocation, a dual-process architecture for parallel QPSK channels inspired by Kahneman's System 1/System 2 framework. A fast numerical optimizer (System 1) continuously performs projected gradient ascent on a weighted mutual-information objective, while an LLM navigator (System 2) periodically interprets natural-language policies and updates only the channel weights and the operational power budget. The LLM never manipulates the power-allocation variables directly, and constraint satisfaction is enforced structurally by the optimizer. To mitigate LLM unreliability, we further incorporate multi-layer guardrails including normalization, exponential moving-average smoothing, and fallback mechanisms. Numerical experiments on an 8-channel system show that, with a fixed optimization core and unchanged system prompt, different natural-language policies induce qualitatively different operating points, including throughput-oriented allocation, channel prioritization, power-aware operation, and channel shutdown. In addition, under an abrupt channel-gain reversal, the proposed system autonomously reconfigures its steering signals and reduces the final mutual-information spread by 60% compared with the optimizer alone. These results suggest that LLMs can serve as policy interpreters for safe, flexible reconfiguration of communication-system optimizers without controller reimplementation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a dual-process architecture (LLM-Steered Power Allocation) for power allocation in parallel QPSK channels, where a fast numerical optimizer handles continuous optimization and an LLM navigator interprets natural-language policies to adjust high-level parameters, with guardrails ensuring safe operation.
AI.motivation: LLMs are stochastic and unreliable, making their safe integration as decision modules in closed-loop systems challenging. The authors aim to leverage LLMs for flexible reconfiguration without compromising safety or requiring controller reimplementation.
AI.method: A dual-process architecture inspired by Kahneman's System 1/System 2: a fast numerical optimizer (System 1) performs projected gradient ascent on weighted mutual-information objective, while an LLM navigator (System 2) interprets natural-language policies to update channel weights and power budget only. Multi-layer guardrails (normalization, EMA smoothing, fallback) ensure safety.
AI.result: On an 8-channel system, different natural-language policies induced qualitatively different operating points. Under abrupt channel-gain reversal, the system autonomously reconfigured and reduced final mutual-information spread by 60% compared to optimizer alone.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can serve as safe policy interpreters for flexible reconfiguration of communication-system optimizers without requiring controller reimplementation.

title: Unlocking Multi-Spectral Data for Multi-Modal Models with Guided Inputs and Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Multi-spectral imagery is a valuable input signal for Remote Sensing applications, such as land-use and land-cover classification and environmental monitoring. However, generalist Large Multi-modal Models (LMMs) are typically trained on RGB images, limiting their applicability to the RGB domain. At the same time, training multi-spectral multi-modal models is expensive and produces uniquely specialized models. To address this, we propose a novel training-free approach that introduces multi-spectral data within the inference pipeline of standard RGB-only LMMs, allowing large gains in performance. Our approach leverages the LMMs' understanding of the visual space by adapting non-RGB inputs to that space and injecting domain-specific information and Chain-of-Thought reasoning as instructions. We demonstrate this with the Gemini 2.5 model and observe strong Zero-Shot performance gains on popular Remote Sensing benchmarks. These results highlight the potential for geospatial professionals to leverage powerful generalist models for specialized sensor inputs, benefiting from rich reasoning capabilities grounded in specialized data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a training-free method to adapt multi-spectral imagery for standard RGB-only Large Multi-modal Models (LMMs), achieving strong Zero-Shot performance gains on Remote Sensing benchmarks without requiring specialized multi-spectral model training.
AI.motivation: Multi-spectral imagery is valuable for Remote Sensing but generalist LMMs are limited to RGB inputs. Training multi-spectral multi-modal models is expensive and produces overly specialized models.
AI.method: A training-free inference pipeline that: (1) adapts non-RGB multi-spectral inputs to the RGB visual space of standard LMMs, (2) injects domain-specific information, and (3) uses Chain-of-Thought reasoning as instructions.
AI.result: Demonstrated with Gemini 2.5, achieving strong Zero-Shot performance gains on popular Remote Sensing benchmarks compared to standard RGB-only LMM baselines.
AI.conclusion: Geospatial professionals can leverage powerful generalist LMMs for specialized multi-spectral sensor inputs without costly retraining, benefiting from rich reasoning capabilities grounded in specialized data.

title: FairyFuse: Multiplication-Free LLM Inference on CPUs via Fused Ternary Kernels
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed on CPU-only platforms where memory bandwidth is the primary bottleneck for autoregressive generation. Weight quantization to four bits or below reduces memory pressure, yet existing systems still dequantize weights and perform floating-point multiplications, limiting the achievable gains. Ternary weights in {-1, 0, +1} provide a more efficient alternative, replacing multiplications with conditional additions, subtractions, or no-ops. While Fairy2i shows that ternary LLMs can match FP16 quality, its runtime does not exploit this structure. We present FairyFuse, an inference system that enables multiplication-free execution on commodity CPUs by fusing the eight real-valued sub-GEMVs of each widely-linear layer into a single AVX-512 loop using masked additions and subtractions, with zero floating-point multiplications. Roofline analysis shows that 16x weight compression shifts memory-bound GEMV toward the compute regime on bandwidth-limited CPUs, yielding a 29.6x kernel speedup while offering little benefit on GPUs. End-to-end, FairyFuse achieves 32.4 tokens per second on a single Intel Xeon 8558P, outperforming llama.cpp Q4_K_M by 1.24x with near-lossless quality (WikiText-2 perplexity 5.52 vs. 5.47 FP16; downstream accuracy 66.0%).

AI.tldr: FairyFuse enables multiplication-free inference of ternary LLMs on CPU by fusing sub-GEMV operations into AVX-512 masked additions/subtractions, achieving 29.6x kernel speedup and 32.4 tokens/s on Intel Xeon 8558P, outperforming llama.cpp Q4_K_M by 1.24x with near-lossless quality.
AI.motivation: LLM inference on CPU is memory-bandwidth-bound. Weight quantization to 4-bit still uses dequantization + floating-point multiplications. Ternary weights ({-1,0,+1}) replace multiplications with additions but prior systems (e.g., Fairy2i) don't exploit this structure.
AI.method: FairyFuse fuses the eight real-valued sub-GEMVs of each linear layer into a single AVX-512 loop using masked additions and subtractions, completely eliminating floating-point multiplications. This leverages 16x weight compression to shift the bottleneck from memory-bound to compute-bound on CPUs.
