AI.result: 29.6x kernel speedup on CPU (limited benefit on GPU). End-to-end 32.4 tokens/s on single Intel Xeon 8558P, 1.24x faster than llama.cpp Q4_K_M. WikiText-2 perplexity 5.52 vs 5.47 (FP16), downstream accuracy 66.0% (near-lossless).
AI.conclusion: Ternary LLM inference can be made multiplication-free on commodity CPUs with significant speedups and minimal quality loss, making it a practical alternative to traditional quantized inference on bandwidth-limited hardware.

title: Value-Conflict Diagnostics Reveal Widespread Alignment Faking in Language Models
comment: Under submission at COLM 2026 Won the Best Student Paper Award at MSLD 2026 @ UIUC
summary: Alignment faking, where a model behaves aligned with developer policy when monitored but reverts to its own preferences when unobserved, is a concerning yet poorly understood phenomenon, in part because current diagnostic tools remain limited. Prior diagnostics rely on highly toxic and clearly harmful scenarios, causing most models to refuse immediately. As a result, models never deliberate over developer policy, monitoring conditions, or the consequences of non-compliance, making these diagnostics fundamentally unable to detect alignment faking propensity. To support study of this phenomenon, we first introduce VLAF, a diagnostic framework grounded in the hypothesis that alignment faking is most likely when developer policy conflicts with a model's strongly held values. VLAF uses morally unambiguous scenarios to probe this conflict across diverse moral values, bypassing refusal behavior while preserving meaningful deliberative stakes. Using VLAF, we find that alignment faking is substantially more prevalent than previously reported, occurring in models as small as 7B parameters - with olmo2-7b-instruct faking alignment in 37% of cases.Finally, we show that oversight conditions induce activation shifts that lie along a single direction in representation space. This means the behavioral divergence driving alignment faking can be captured by a single contrastive steering vector, which we exploit for lightweight inference-time mitigation. Finally, we exploit this for mitigation that requires no labeled data and minimal computational overhead, achieving relative reductions in alignment faking of 85.8%, 94.0%, and 57.7% on olmo2-7b-instruct, olmo2-13b-instruct, and qwen3-8b respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VLAF, a diagnostic framework to detect alignment faking in LLMs, finding it is more prevalent than previously reported (e.g., 37% in olmo2-7b-instruct). It also proposes a lightweight mitigation method using contrastive steering vectors, reducing alignment faking by up to 94%.
AI.motivation: Prior diagnostics for alignment faking rely on highly toxic scenarios that cause model refusal, preventing detection of alignment faking propensity. Better tools are needed to study this phenomenon.
AI.method: Proposes VLAF, which uses morally unambiguous scenarios to probe conflicts between developer policy and model values, bypassing refusal. Also identifies a single contrastive steering vector in representation space for mitigation.
AI.result: Alignment faking is more prevalent than previously reported, occurring in models as small as 7B parameters (37% for olmo2-7b-instruct). Mitigation reduces faking by 85.8%, 94.0%, and 57.7% across tested models.
AI.conclusion: VLAF enables detection of alignment faking, and a lightweight, zero-data mitigation method effectively reduces it. This opens pathways for safer model deployment.

title: Self-Predictive Representation for Autonomous UAV Object-Goal Navigation
summary: Autonomous Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have revolutionized industries through their versatility with applications including aerial surveillance, search and rescue, agriculture, and delivery. Their autonomous capabilities offer unique advantages, such as operating in large open space environments. Reinforcement Learning (RL) empowers UAVs to learn intricate navigation policies, enabling them to optimize flight behavior autonomously. However, one of its main challenge is the inefficiency in using data sample to achieve a good policy. In object-goal navigation (OGN) settings, target recognition arises as an extra challenge. Most UAV-related approaches use relative or absolute coordinates to move from an initial position to a predefined location, rather than to find the target directly. This study addresses the data sample efficiency issue in solving a 3D OGN problem, in addition to, the formalization of the unknown target location setting as a Markov decision process. Experiments are conducted to analyze the interplay of different state representation learning (SRL) methods for perception with a model-free RL algorithm for planning in an autonomous navigation system. The main contribution of this study is the development of the perception module, featuring a novel self-predictive model named AmelPred. Empirical results demonstrate that its stochastic version, AmelPredSto, is the best-performing SRL model when combined with actor-critic RL algorithms. The obtained results show substantial improvement in RL algorithms' efficiency by using AmelPredSto in solving the OGN problem.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes AmelPred, a novel self-predictive state representation learning model (with its stochastic variant AmelPredSto being the best), to improve data sample efficiency in 3D object-goal navigation for autonomous UAVs using reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement Learning for UAV navigation suffers from data sample inefficiency, and in object-goal navigation settings, target recognition adds extra challenges. Existing approaches typically use coordinates to predefined locations rather than directly finding targets.
AI.method: The study formalizes the unknown target location setting as a Markov decision process, develops a perception module with a novel self-predictive model (AmelPred), and combines it with model-free actor-critic RL algorithms. Experiments compare different SRL methods for perception.
AI.result: The stochastic version AmelPredSto is the best-performing SRL model when combined with actor-critic RL algorithms, showing substantial improvement in RL algorithms' data efficiency for solving the object-goal navigation problem.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AmelPredSto model effectively addresses data sample efficiency and target recognition challenges in 3D OGN for autonomous UAVs, significantly enhancing RL-based navigation performance.

title: HyperCEUNet: Parameter-Aware Hypernetwork-Driven UNet for Channel Estimation
summary: Deep learning-based channel estimation has been recognized as a promising technique for sixth-generation wireless systems. However, most existing approaches rely solely on least-squares estimates obtained from demodulation reference signals, which fail to explicitly exploit channel time-frequency correlation parameters. Inspired by the independent channel parameter estimation enabled by semi-static reference signals in modern wireless systems, this letter presents a parameter-aware deep learning-based channel estimation framework termed HyperCEUNet. Specifically, the proposed hypernetwork generates an adaptive front-end convolutional layer based on estimated channel parameters, serving as a pre-filtering stage before the UNet-based estimator. In addition, the Wiener-filtered channel estimates are adopted to provide a correlation-aware initialization for data resources. Simulation results demonstrate that our proposed HyperCEUNet effectively improves channel estimation accuracy compared with its conventional counterparts.

AI.tldr: HyperCEUNet: A deep learning channel estimation framework that uses hypernetworks to generate adaptive convolutional layers based on semi-static reference signal parameters, combined with Wiener-filtered initialization, improving estimation accuracy for 6G wireless systems.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning channel estimation methods rely on least-squares estimates from demodulation reference signals, failing to explicitly exploit channel time-frequency correlation parameters.
AI.method: HyperCEUNet uses a hypernetwork to generate an adaptive front-end convolutional layer based on estimated channel parameters (from semi-static reference signals) as a pre-filtering stage before a UNet-based estimator, and adopts Wiener-filtered channel estimates for correlation-aware initialization.
AI.result: Simulation results show that HyperCEUNet effectively improves channel estimation accuracy compared to conventional counterparts.
AI.conclusion: Parameter-aware deep learning (via hypernetworks and Wiener-filtered initialization) outperforms conventional approaches for channel estimation in 6G wireless systems.

title: Design of MDP Convolutional Codes and Maximally Recoverable Codes Through the Lens of Matrix Completion
summary: The matrix completion problem provides a unifying lens through which many fundamental problems in coding theory can be viewed. In this paper, we investigate Locally Recoverable Codes (LRCs) with Maximal Recoverability (MR) and Maximum Distance Profile (MDP) convolutional codes in the framework of matrix completion. In particular, we present techniques that are general enough to provide constructions for both types of codes. A common feature of our code constructions is the sparsity of their generator matrices and the property that a large number of the entries of the generator matrices are elements of a small subfield of a larger extension field.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates locally recoverable codes (LRCs) with maximal recoverability (MR) and maximum distance profile (MDP) convolutional codes through the lens of matrix completion, presenting general construction techniques that yield sparse generator matrices with entries from a small subfield.
AI.motivation: To unify the understanding of locally recoverable codes and MDP convolutional codes under the matrix completion framework, and to provide general construction techniques for both types of codes.
AI.method: The paper uses matrix completion as a unifying framework and presents general techniques for constructing both LRCs with maximal recoverability and MDP convolutional codes, with a focus on achieving sparsity in generator matrices and using elements from a small subfield.
AI.result: Construction techniques that produce codes with sparse generator matrices, where a large number of entries belong to a small subfield of a larger extension field.
AI.conclusion: The matrix completion framework enables unified constructions for both MR-LRCs and MDP convolutional codes, with the advantage of sparsity and subfield properties.

title: Projected Gradient Unlearning for Text-to-Image Diffusion Models: Defending Against Concept Revival Attacks
summary: Machine unlearning for text-to-image diffusion models aims to selectively remove undesirable concepts from pre-trained models without costly retraining. Current unlearning methods share a common weakness: erased concepts return when the model is fine-tuned on downstream data, even when that data is entirely unrelated. We adapt Projected Gradient Unlearning (PGU) from classification to the diffusion domain as a post-hoc hardening step. By constructing a Core Gradient Space (CGS) from the retain concept activations and projecting gradient updates into its orthogonal complement, PGU ensures that subsequent fine-tuning cannot undo the achieved erasure. Applied on top of existing methods (ESD, UCE, Receler), the approach eliminates revival for style concepts and substantially delays it for object concepts, running in roughly 6 minutes versus the ~2 hours required by Meta-Unlearning. PGU and Meta-Unlearning turn out to be complementary: which performs better depends on how the concept is encoded, and retain concept selection should follow visual feature similarity rather than semantic grouping.

AI.tldr: PGU adapts projected gradient unlearning to diffusion models, preventing erased concepts from reviving during fine-tuning by projecting gradient updates orthogonal to a Core Gradient Space, achieving fast (~6 min) protection without costly retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing unlearning methods for text-to-image diffusion models fail to permanently erase undesirable concepts—erased concepts reappear when the model is fine-tuned on downstream data, even unrelated data.
AI.method: Adapts Projected Gradient Unlearning (PGU) from classification to diffusion models by constructing a Core Gradient Space (CGS) from retain concept activations and projecting fine-tuning gradient updates into its orthogonal complement, preventing erasure reversal.
AI.result: PGU completely eliminates revival for style concepts and substantially delays it for object concepts. PGU runs in ~6 minutes versus ~2 hours for Meta-Unlearning. PGU and Meta-Unlearning are complementary, with performance depending on concept encoding.
AI.conclusion: PGU is a fast, effective post-hoc hardening step for diffusion model unlearning, and retain concept selection should follow visual feature similarity rather than semantic grouping.

title: Absorber LLM: Harnessing Causal Synchronization for Test-Time Training
summary: Transformers suffer from a high computational cost that grows with sequence length for self-attention, making inference in long streams prohibited by memory consumption. Constant-memory alternatives such as RNNs and SSMs compress history into states with fixed size and thus lose long-tail dependencies, while methods that memorize contexts into parameters, such as Test-Time Training (TTT), are prone to overfitting token-level projection and fail to preserve the causal effect of context in pretrained LLMs. We propose Absorber LLM, which formulates long-context retention as a self-supervised causal synchronization: after absorbing historical contexts into parameters, a contextless model should match the original model with full context on future generations. We optimize this objective by synchronizing internal behaviors of the updated model with the original one, ensuring context absorption and generalization. Experiments on long-context and streaming benchmarks show that Absorber LLM reduces inference memory and improves accuracy over prior parameter-as-memory baselines.

AI.tldr: Absorber LLM introduces a self-supervised causal synchronization method that absorbs long contexts into model parameters, reducing memory costs while preserving long-tail dependencies better than RNNs/SSMs and avoiding overfitting issues in Test-Time Training methods.
AI.motivation: Transformers have high computational cost for long sequences due to self-attention; RNNs/SSMs compress history into fixed-size states losing long-tail dependencies; Test-Time Training methods overfit token-level projections and fail to preserve causal context effects in pretrained LLMs.
AI.method: Formulates long-context retention as self-supervised causal synchronization: after absorbing historical contexts into parameters, a contextless model should match the original model with full context on future generations. This is optimized by synchronizing internal behaviors of the updated model with the original model.
AI.result: Reduces inference memory and improves accuracy over prior parameter-as-memory baselines on long-context and streaming benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Absorber LLM effectively addresses the trade-off between memory efficiency and long-context retention by using causal synchronization to absorb context into parameters without overfitting.

title: The Last Harness You'll Ever Build
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed on complex, domain-specific workflows -- navigating enterprise web applications that require dozens of clicks and form fills, orchestrating multi-step research pipelines that span search, extraction, and synthesis, automating code review across unfamiliar repositories, and handling customer escalations that demand nuanced domain knowledge. \textbf{Each new task domain requires painstaking, expert-driven harness engineering}: designing the prompts, tools, orchestration logic, and evaluation criteria that make a foundation model effective. We present a two-level framework that automates this process. At the first level, the \textbf{Harness Evolution Loop} optimizes a worker agent's harness $\mathcal{H}$ for a single task: a Worker Agent $W_{\mathcal{H}}$ executes the task, an Evaluator Agent $V$ adversarially diagnoses failures and scores performance, and an Evolution Agent $E$ modifies the harness based on the full history of prior attempts. At the second level, the \textbf{Meta-Evolution Loop} optimizes the evolution protocol $Λ= (W_{\mathcal{H}}, \mathcal{H}^{(0)}, V, E)$ itself across diverse tasks, \textbf{learning a protocol $Λ^{(\text{best})}$ that enables rapid harness convergence on any new task -- so that adapting an agent to a novel domain requires no human harness engineering at all.} We formalize the correspondence to meta-learning and present both algorithms. The framework \textbf{shifts manual harness engineering into automated harness engineering}, and takes one step further -- \textbf{automating the design of the automation itself}.

AI.tldr: A two-level automated framework (Harness Evolution Loop + Meta-Evolution Loop) that eliminates manual harness engineering for AI agents, enabling zero-human adaptation to new domains by learning an optimal evolution protocol across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Deploying AI agents to new domain-specific workflows currently requires painstaking, expert-driven manual harness engineering (prompts, tools, orchestration, evaluation criteria). The authors aim to eliminate this human bottleneck.
AI.method: Two-level framework: (1) Harness Evolution Loop optimizes a worker agent's harness for a single task using a Worker (executes), Evaluator (diagnoses/scores), and Evolution agent (modifies harness). (2) Meta-Evolution Loop optimizes the evolution protocol itself across diverse tasks, learning a protocol that enables rapid harness convergence on any new task.
AI.result: The framework automates harness engineering, and further automates the design of the automation itself — enabling adaptation to novel domains without any human harness engineering.
AI.conclusion: The work formalizes the correspondence to meta-learning, presents algorithms for both loops, and shifts manual harness engineering into automated harness engineering, taking a step toward fully autonomous agent adaptation.

title: Navigating the Clutter: Waypoint-Based Bi-Level Planning for Multi-Robot Systems
summary: Multi-robot control in cluttered environments is a challenging problem that involves complex physical constraints, including robot-robot collisions, robot-obstacle collisions, and unreachable motions. Successful planning in such settings requires joint optimization over high-level task planning and low-level motion planning, as violations of physical constraints may arise from failures at either level. However, jointly optimizing task and motion planning is difficult due to the complex parameterization of low-level motion trajectories and the ambiguity of credit assignment across the two planning levels. In this paper, we propose a hybrid multi-robot control framework that jointly optimizes task and motion planning. To enable effective parameterization of low-level planning, we introduce waypoints, a simple yet expressive representation for motion trajectories. To address the credit assignment challenge, we adopt a curriculum-based training strategy with a modified RLVR algorithm that propagates motion feasibility feedback from the motion planner to the task planner. Experiments on BoxNet3D-OBS, a challenging multi-robot benchmark with dense obstacles and up to nine robots, show that our approach consistently improves task success over motion-agnostic and VLA-based baselines. Our code is available at https://github.com/UCSB-NLP-Chang/navigate-cluster

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hybrid multi-robot control framework that jointly optimizes task and motion planning in cluttered environments, using waypoints for motion trajectory representation and a curriculum-based RLVR training strategy for credit assignment. Experiments on the BoxNet3D-OBS benchmark show improved task success over baselines.
AI.motivation: Multi-robot control in cluttered environments requires joint optimization over high-level task planning and low-level motion planning, but this is challenging due to complex parameterization of motion trajectories and ambiguity of credit assignment across the two levels.
AI.method: Proposes a hybrid framework with (1) waypoints as a simple yet expressive representation for motion trajectories, and (2) a curriculum-based training strategy with a modified RLVR algorithm that propagates motion feasibility feedback from the motion planner to the task planner.
AI.result: On the BoxNet3D-OBS benchmark (dense obstacles, up to 9 robots), the approach consistently improves task success over motion-agnostic and VLA-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of task and motion planning via waypoint representation and curriculum-based RLVR with feasibility feedback effectively addresses multi-robot control in cluttered environments.

title: Scalable Multimodal Beam Alignment in V2X: An Anti-Imbalance Graph Learning Approach
summary: Efficient beam alignment is fundamental to high-throughput and reliable connectivity in Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) systems. However, conventional beam management in dynamic vehicular topologies incurs prohibitive alignment overhead and struggles to maintain robust links under rapid mobility. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a distributed multimodal graph beam alignment (GBA) framework. The core innovation lies in leveraging onboard multimodal sensing data to predict implicit feedback while employing graph neural networks to coordinate multi-user alignment, thereby jointly enhancing scalability and drastically reducing overhead. The architecture adopts a dual-network design with GBA-RSU and GBA-Vehicle units, optimized through a hybrid strategy of centralized learning and federated learning (FL) to balance global performance with local privacy. Furthermore, a dedicated data augmentation (DA) scheme is introduced to address multimodal data imbalance issues in vehicular networks. Negative augmentation applies dominant modality dropout to bolster robustness, while positive augmentation generates underrepresented samples to mitigate label imbalance. Numerical results demonstrate that GBA maintains a competitive sum rate on par with high-resolution codebook-based feedback yet reduces beam alignment overhead by over 90\% and scales efficiently in mobile scenarios. Notably, integrating DA enables GBA to consistently outperform state-of-the-art FL-based alignment benchmarks, with particularly pronounced gains under severe label and modality imbalance, establishing a practical solution for V2X beam management.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a distributed multimodal graph beam alignment (GBA) framework for V2X systems that leverages multimodal sensing data and graph neural networks to reduce beam alignment overhead by over 90% while maintaining competitive performance, using a hybrid centralized-federated learning approach with data augmentation to address data imbalance.
AI.motivation: Conventional beam management in dynamic vehicular networks incurs prohibitive alignment overhead and struggles to maintain robust links under rapid mobility.
AI.method: A distributed multimodal GBA framework with dual-network design (GBA-RSU and GBA-Vehicle units), hybrid centralized learning and federated learning strategy, and a dedicated data augmentation scheme with negative (dominant modality dropout) and positive (generating underrepresented samples) augmentation.
AI.result: GBA maintains competitive sum rate on par with high-resolution codebook-based feedback, reduces beam alignment overhead by over 90%, scales efficiently in mobile scenarios, and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art FL-based alignment benchmarks, especially under severe label and modality imbalance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GBA framework with DA establishes a practical solution for V2X beam management by drastically reducing overhead while ensuring robust performance and scalability.

title: Robust Beamforming for MIMO Radar with Imperfect Prior Distribution Information
comment: Accepted to appear in IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory (ISIT), 2026
summary: This paper studies a multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) radar system for sensing the unknown and random angular location (angle) of a point target, based on the target-reflected echo signals and known prior distribution information about the target's angle specified by a probability density function (PDF). We consider a challenging yet practical scenario where the knowledge of such PDF is imperfect, due to the inaccuracy in PDF acquisition or unpredicted change of target appearance pattern; while the real (actual) PDF is modeled as an unknown perturbed version of the imperfect known PDF bounded by a given uncertainty radius. Such PDF imperfection motivates us to study the robust transmit beamforming design to optimize the worst-case sensing performance among all possible real PDFs. Since the sensing mean-squared error (MSE) is difficult to be characterized explicitly, we adopt the worst-case posterior Cramér-Rao bound (PCRB) as the performance metric. We formulate the beamforming optimization problem to minimize the maximum PCRB among all possible real PDFs, which is highly non-trivial since the PCRB has a complex intractable expression over the real PDF, and there are infinite constraints corresponding to the continuous set of real PDFs bounded by the uncertainty radius. To address these challenges, we derive a tractable quadratic approximation of the PCRB via second-order Taylor expansion, and leverage the S-procedure to equivalently transform the infinite constraints into a linear matrix inequality, based on which the problem is reformulated into a convex optimization problem solvable with polynomial time complexity. The obtained solution approaches the globally optimal robust beamforming solution as the uncertainty radius decreases. Numerical results validate the effectiveness of our proposed robust beamforming design.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a robust transmit beamforming design for MIMO radar to minimize the worst-case posterior Cramér-Rao bound (PCRB) when the prior probability density function (PDF) of the target's angle is imperfect and only known within an uncertainty radius.
AI.motivation: In practical MIMO radar sensing, the prior distribution of the target's angle is often imperfectly known due to acquisition errors or unpredictable changes. This PDF imperfection can degrade sensing performance, motivating a robust design that accounts for worst-case scenarios.
AI.method: The authors use the worst-case posterior Cramér-Rao bound (PCRB) as the performance metric. They derive a tractable quadratic approximation of the PCRB via second-order Taylor expansion and use the S-procedure to transform infinite constraints into a linear matrix inequality, reformulating the problem as a convex optimization problem solvable in polynomial time.
AI.result: The proposed approach yields a solution that approaches the globally optimal robust beamforming design as the uncertainty radius decreases. Numerical results validate the effectiveness of the robust beamforming design.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the challenging robust beamforming problem under PDF uncertainty by deriving a tractable convex formulation, enabling efficient and effective worst-case sensing performance optimization for MIMO radar.

title: StyleVAR: Controllable Image Style Transfer via Visual Autoregressive Modeling
summary: We build on the Visual Autoregressive Modeling (VAR) framework and formulate style transfer as conditional discrete sequence modeling in a learned latent space. Images are decomposed into multi-scale representations and tokenized into discrete codes by a VQ-VAE; a transformer then autoregressively models the distribution of target tokens conditioned on style and content tokens. To inject style and content information, we introduce a blended cross-attention mechanism in which the evolving target representation attends to its own history, while style and content features act as queries that decide which aspects of this history to emphasize. A scale-dependent blending coefficient controls the relative influence of style and content at each stage, encouraging the synthesized representation to align with both the content structure and the style texture without breaking the autoregressive continuity of VAR. We train StyleVAR in two stages from a pretrained VAR checkpoint: supervised fine-tuning on a large triplet dataset of content--style--target images, followed by reinforcement fine-tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) against a DreamSim-based perceptual reward, with per-action normalization weighting to rebalance credit across VAR's multi-scale hierarchy. Across three benchmarks spanning in-, near-, and out-of-distribution regimes, StyleVAR consistently outperforms an AdaIN baseline on Style Loss, Content Loss, LPIPS, SSIM, DreamSim, and CLIP similarity, and the GRPO stage yields further gains over the SFT checkpoint, most notably on the reward-aligned perceptual metrics. Qualitatively, the method transfers texture while maintaining semantic structure, especially for landscapes and architectural scenes, while a generalization gap on internet images and difficulty with human faces highlight the need for better content diversity and stronger structural priors.

AI.tldr: StyleVAR formulates style transfer as autoregressive discrete sequence modeling using VQ-VAE tokens, with blended cross-attention for style/content control and two-stage training (SFT + GRPO), outperforming AdaIN across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing style transfer methods struggle to simultaneously preserve content structure and transfer style texture, and autoregressive models like VAR have not been effectively adapted for conditional style transfer tasks.
AI.method: StyleVAR decomposes images into multi-scale VQ-VAE tokens, uses a transformer with blended cross-attention (style/content as queries), and trains via supervised fine-tuning followed by GRPO with DreamSim-based perceptual reward and per-action normalization weighting.
AI.result: Outperforms AdaIN on Style Loss, Content Loss, LPIPS, SSIM, DreamSim, and CLIP similarity across in-, near-, and out-of-distribution benchmarks; GRPO stage further improves perceptual metrics.
AI.conclusion: StyleVAR effectively transfers texture while preserving semantic structure for landscapes and architecture, but exhibits generalization gaps on internet images and faces, indicating need for better content diversity and structural priors.

title: The Path Not Taken: Duality in Reasoning about Program Execution
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities across diverse coding tasks. However, their adoption requires a true understanding of program execution rather than relying on surface-level patterns. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on predicting program properties tied to specific inputs (e.g., code coverage, program outputs). As a result, they provide a narrow view of dynamic code reasoning and are prone to data contamination. We argue that understanding program execution requires evaluating its inherent duality through two complementary reasoning tasks: (i) predicting a program's observed behavior for a given input, and (ii) inferring how the input must be mutated toward a specific behavioral objective. Both tasks jointly probe a model's causal understanding of execution flow. We instantiate this duality in DexBench, a benchmark comprising 445 paired instances, and evaluate 13 LLMs. Our results demonstrate that dual-path reasoning provides a robust and discriminative proxy for dynamic code understanding.

AI.tldr: DexBench is a benchmark with 445 paired instances that evaluates LLMs' program understanding through dual reasoning tasks: predicting program behavior for given inputs and inferring input mutations toward behavioral objectives, testing causal understanding beyond surface patterns.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks only evaluate narrow aspects of dynamic code reasoning (e.g., predicting program outputs) and are prone to data contamination, failing to assess models' true causal understanding of program execution.
AI.method: The authors propose dual-path reasoning with two complementary tasks: (1) predicting observed behavior for a given input, and (2) inferring input mutations for a specific behavioral objective. They instantiate this in DexBench with 445 paired instances and evaluate 13 LLMs.
AI.result: Dual-path reasoning serves as a robust and discriminative proxy for dynamic code understanding, better differentiating model capabilities than single-task evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating program understanding requires both directions of reasoning (behavior prediction and input mutation) to assess causal comprehension of execution flow, and DexBench effectively measures this.

title: Deep FinResearch Bench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Conduct Professional Financial Investment Research
summary: We introduce Deep FinResearch Bench, a practical and comprehensive evaluation framework for deep research (DR) agents in financial investment research. The benchmark assesses three dimensions of report quality: qualitative rigor, quantitative forecasting and valuation accuracy, and claim credibility and verifiability. Particularly, we define corresponding qualitative and quantitative evaluation metrics and implement an automated scoring procedure to enable scalable assessment. Applying the benchmark to financial reports from frontier DR agents and comparing them with reports authored by financial professionals, we find that AI-generated reports still fall short across these dimensions. These findings underscore the need for domain-specialized DR agents tailored to finance, and we hope the work establishes a foundation for standardized benchmarking of DR agents in financial research.

AI.tldr: Deep FinResearch Bench is a new evaluation framework for AI deep research agents in financial investment, testing reports across qualitative rigor, quantitative accuracy, and claim verifiability. Results show AI-generated reports still underperform compared to human financial professionals.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of standardized benchmarks for evaluating deep research agents in the specialized domain of financial investment research, where report quality involves multiple dimensions beyond simple factual accuracy.
AI.method: The authors define qualitative and quantitative evaluation metrics across three dimensions (qualitative rigor, quantitative forecasting/valuation accuracy, claim credibility/verifiability) and implement an automated scoring procedure for scalable assessment.
AI.result: AI-generated reports from frontier deep research agents fall short of human-authored financial reports across all three evaluated dimensions.
AI.conclusion: There is a need for domain-specialized deep research agents tailored to finance, and this benchmark provides a foundation for standardized evaluation in financial research.

title: Full-Body Dynamic Safety for Robot Manipulators: 3D Poisson Safety Functions for CBF-Based Safety Filters
summary: Collision avoidance for robotic manipulators requires enforcing full-body safety constraints in high-dimensional configuration spaces. Control Barrier Function (CBF) based safety filters have proven effective in enabling safe behaviors, but enforcing the high number of constraints needed for safe manipulation leads to theoretic and computational challenges. This work presents a framework for full-body collision avoidance for manipulators in dynamic environments by leveraging 3D Poisson Safety Functions (PSFs). In particular, given environmental occupancy data, we sample the manipulator surface at a prescribed resolution and shrink free space via a Pontryagin difference according to this resolution. On this buffered domain, we synthesize a globally smooth CBF by solving Poisson's equation, yielding a single safety function for the entire environment. This safety function, evaluated at each sampled point, yields task-space CBF constraints enforced by a real-time safety filter via a multi-constraint quadratic program. We prove that keeping the sample points safe in the buffered region guarantees collision avoidance for the entire continuous robot surface. The framework is validated on a 7-degree-of-freedom manipulator in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework for full-body collision avoidance of robotic manipulators using 3D Poisson Safety Functions (PSFs) that synthesize a single smooth Control Barrier Function (CBF) via solving Poisson's equation on a buffered domain, enabling real-time safety filtering through a multi-constraint quadratic program.
AI.motivation: Existing CBF-based safety filters face theoretical and computational challenges when enforcing the high number of constraints needed for safe full-body manipulation in dynamic environments.
AI.method: The method samples the manipulator surface at a prescribed resolution, shrinks free space via Pontryagin difference according to this resolution, then synthesizes a globally smooth CBF by solving Poisson's equation to produce a single safety function for the entire environment.
AI.result: The framework is validated on a 7-degree-of-freedom manipulator in dynamic environments, and it is proven that keeping sample points safe in the buffered region guarantees collision avoidance for the entire continuous robot surface.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PSF-based approach effectively addresses the theoretical and computational challenges of full-body collision avoidance for manipulators, offering a single smooth safety function that simplifies real-time enforcement.

title: Event-Triggered Distributed Target Tracking via PRIMEX
summary: PRIMEX (prime-based graph encoding and extraction) is a recently proposed framework for scalable distributed fusion. In PRIMEX, the information pedigree of state estimates or probability density functions is encoded using the information codes, enabling lightweight arithmetic for redundancy removal and data integration. Building on PRIMEX and its memoryless fusion strategy based on a least-squares approximation, in this paper we present two efficient distributed tracking algorithms: a consensus-based PRIMEX method that fuses information from all neighbors, and a greedy gossip-based PRIMEX method that fuses with the most informative neighbor. To further increase communication efficiency, we incorporate an event-triggered mechanism, in which transmission decisions are driven by information novelty measured using differences between the information codes. The proposed methods are evaluated and compared with covariance intersection and centralized fusion in a distributed single target tracking scenario. Simulation results show that PRIMEX-based methods remain competitive in tracking accuracy while improving communication efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper extends PRIMEX (a scalable distributed fusion framework) with two efficient distributed tracking algorithms—consensus-based and greedy gossip-based—and an event-triggered mechanism that uses information code differences to improve communication efficiency, achieving competitive tracking accuracy compared to covariance intersection and centralized fusion.
AI.motivation: To improve communication efficiency in distributed tracking while maintaining competitive accuracy, building on the PRIMEX framework's information pedigree encoding and memoryless fusion strategy.
AI.method: Two algorithms are proposed: (1) a consensus-based PRIMEX method fusing information from all neighbors, and (2) a greedy gossip-based PRIMEX method fusing with the most informative neighbor. An event-triggered mechanism using information code differences is incorporated to further reduce communication.
AI.result: Simulation results in a distributed single target tracking scenario show PRIMEX-based methods remain competitive in tracking accuracy while improving communication efficiency compared to covariance intersection and centralized fusion.
AI.conclusion: PRIMEX-based distributed tracking methods effectively balance accuracy and communication efficiency, offering a promising approach for scalable distributed fusion.

title: Generative Learning Enhanced Intelligent Resource Management for Cell-Free Delay Deterministic Communications
comment: The paper has been submitted to IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
summary: Cell-free multiple-input multiple-output (CF-MIMO) architecture significantly enhances wireless network performance, offering a promising solution for delay-sensitive applications. This paper investigates the resource allocation problem in CF-MIMO systems, aiming to maximize energy efficiency (EE) while satisfying delay violation rate constraint. We design a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with a primal-dual method to solve it. To address the low sample efficiency and safety risks caused by cold-start of the designed safe deep reinforcement learning (DRL) method, we propose a novel offline pretraining framework based on virtual constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) modeling. The virtual CMDP consists of reward and cost prediction module, initial-state distribution module and state transition module. Notably, we propose an evidence-aware conditional Gaussian Mixture Model (EA-CGMM) inference approach to mitigate data sparsity and distribution drift issues in state transition modeling. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of CMDP modeling and validate the safety and efficiency of the proposed pretraining framework. Specifically, compared with non-pretrained baseline, the agent pretrained through our proposed framework achieves twice the initial EE and maintains a low delay constraint violation rate of $1\%$, while ultimately converging to an EE that is $4.7\%$ higher with a $50\%$ reduction in exploration steps. Additionally, our proposed pretraining framework implementation exhibits comparable performance to the SOTA diffusion model-based implementation, while achieving a $14$-fold reduction in computational complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an offline pretraining framework using virtual CMDP modeling and EA-CGMM for safe DRL in CF-MIMO systems, achieving higher energy efficiency, lower delay violations, faster convergence, and 14x complexity reduction compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: To maximize energy efficiency in CF-MIMO systems under delay constraints, while overcoming cold-start safety risks and low sample efficiency in safe DRL methods.
AI.method: A PPO with primal-dual method is designed, enhanced by an offline pretraining framework based on virtual CMDP modeling. The virtual CMDP includes reward/cost prediction, initial-state distribution, and state transition modules. An EA-CGMM inference approach handles data sparsity and distribution drift in state transition modeling.
AI.result: Pretrained agent achieves twice the initial EE, 1% delay violation rate, 4.7% higher final EE, 50% fewer exploration steps, and 14x computational complexity reduction over the diffusion model-based SOTA.
AI.conclusion: The proposed offline pretraining framework significantly improves safety and efficiency of DRL-based resource allocation in CF-MIMO systems, offering a practical and computationally efficient solution for delay-sensitive applications.

title: Clinically-Informed Modeling for Pediatric Brain Tumor Classification from Whole-Slide Histopathology Images
comment: Accepted at the IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI), 2026
summary: Accurate diagnosis of pediatric brain tumors, starting with histopathology, presents unique challenges for deep learning, including severe data scarcity, class imbalance, and fine-grained morphologic overlap across diagnostically distinct subtypes. While pathology foundation models have advanced patch-level representation learning, their effective adaptation to weakly supervised pediatric brain tumor classification under limited data remains underexplored. In this work, we introduce an expert-guided contrastive fine-tuning framework for pediatric brain tumor diagnosis from whole-slide images (WSI). Our approach integrates contrastive learning into slide-level multiple instance learning (MIL) to explicitly regularize the geometry of slide-level representations during downstream fine-tuning. We propose both a general supervised contrastive setting and an expert-guided variant that incorporates clinically informed hard negatives targeting diagnostically confusable subtypes. Through comprehensive experiments on pediatric brain tumor WSI classification under realistic low-sample and class-imbalanced conditions, we demonstrate that contrastive fine-tuning yields measurable improvements in fine-grained diagnostic distinctions. Our experimental analyses reveal complementary strengths across different contrastive strategies, with expert-guided hard negatives promoting more compact intra-class representations and improved inter-class separation. This work highlights the importance of explicitly shaping slide-level representations for robust fine-grained classification in data-scarce pediatric pathology settings.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an expert-guided contrastive fine-tuning framework for pediatric brain tumor classification from whole-slide images, integrating contrastive learning into multiple instance learning to improve fine-grained diagnostic accuracy under data-scarce conditions.
AI.motivation: Pediatric brain tumor diagnosis faces unique challenges including severe data scarcity, class imbalance, and fine-grained morphologic overlap across subtypes. Existing pathology foundation models lack effective adaptation for weakly supervised classification under limited data.
AI.method: The method integrates contrastive learning into slide-level multiple instance learning (MIL) to regularize the geometry of slide-level representations. It proposes both a general supervised contrastive setting and an expert-guided variant with clinically informed hard negatives targeting diagnostically confusable subtypes.
AI.result: Experiments on pediatric brain tumor WSI classification under low-sample and class-imbalanced conditions show measurable improvements in fine-grained diagnostic distinctions. Expert-guided hard negatives yield more compact intra-class representations and improved inter-class separation.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly shaping slide-level representations is important for robust fine-grained classification in data-scarce pediatric pathology settings.

title: Forget, Then Recall: Learnable Compression and Selective Unfolding via Gist Sparse Attention
summary: Scaling large language models to long contexts is challenging due to the quadratic computational cost of full attention. Mitigation approaches include KV-cache selection or compression techniques. We instead provide an effective and end-to-end learnable bridge between the two without requiring architecture modification. In particular, our key insight is that interleaved gist compression tokens -- which provide a learnable summary of sets of raw tokens -- can serve as routing signals for sparse attention. Building on this, we introduce selective unfolding via GSA, which first compresses the context into gist tokens, then selects the most relevant gists, and subsequently restores the corresponding raw chunks for detailed attention. This yields a simple coarse-to-fine mechanism that combines compact global representations with targeted access to fine-grained evidence. We further incorporate this process directly into training in an end-to-end fashion, avoiding the need for external retrieval modules. In addition, we extend the framework hierarchically via recursive gist-of-gist construction, enabling multi-resolution context access with logarithmic per-step decoding complexity. Empirical results on LongBench and RAG benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms other compression baselines as well as inference-time sparse attention methods across compression ratios from $8\times$ to $32\times$. The code is available at: https://github.com/yuzhenmao/gist-sparse-attention/

AI.tldr: This paper proposes GSA (Gist-guided Sparse Attention), an end-to-end learnable method that uses interleaved gist compression tokens as routing signals for sparse attention, achieving multi-resolution context access with logarithmic decoding complexity and outperforming baselines on LongBench and RAG benchmarks at 8x-32x compression ratios.
AI.motivation: Scaling LLMs to long contexts is hindered by the quadratic cost of full attention. Existing mitigation approaches (KV-cache selection or compression) are separate paradigms; the paper aims to bridge them with a unified, end-to-end learnable solution without architectural changes.
AI.method: Introduces selective unfolding via GSA: (1) compress context into interleaved gist tokens via learnable summarization, (2) select most relevant gists as routing signals, (3) restore corresponding raw chunks for fine-grained attention. Extended hierarchically via recursive gist-of-gist construction for multi-resolution access with logarithmic decoding complexity.
AI.result: Outperforms compression baselines and inference-time sparse attention methods on LongBench and RAG benchmarks across compression ratios from 8x to 32x.
AI.conclusion: GSA provides an effective bridge between KV-cache selection and compression, enabling efficient long-context processing with end-to-end training and hierarchical multi-resolution access.

title: Adaptive Test-Time Compute Allocation with Evolving In-Context Demonstrations
summary: While scaling test-time compute can substantially improve model performance, existing approaches either rely on static compute allocation or sample from fixed generation distributions. In this work, we introduce a test-time compute allocation framework that jointly adapts where computation is spent and how generation is performed. Our method begins with a warm-up phase that identifies easy queries and assembles an initial pool of question-response pairs from the test set itself. An adaptive phase then concentrates further computation on unresolved queries while reshaping their generation distributions through evolving in-context demonstrations -- conditioning each generation on successful responses from semantically related queries rather than resampling from a fixed distribution. Experiments across math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing baselines while consuming substantially less inference-time compute.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an adaptive test-time compute allocation framework that identifies easy queries, focuses computation on hard ones, and dynamically reshapes generation distributions using in-context demonstrations from successful related queries, outperforming static baselines with less compute.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time compute methods either allocate compute statically or sample from fixed generation distributions, failing to adapt where and how computation is spent based on query difficulty and prior outcomes.
AI.method: A two-phase framework: (1) Warm-up phase identifies easy queries and builds an initial pool of question-response pairs from the test set. (2) Adaptive phase concentrates compute on unresolved queries, reshaping generation distributions via evolving in-context demonstrations from semantically related successful responses.
AI.result: The approach consistently outperforms existing baselines across math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks while consuming substantially less inference-time compute.
AI.conclusion: Jointly adapting where computation is allocated and how generation distributions are shaped yields better efficiency and performance than static or fixed-distribution test-time compute methods.

title: How VLAs (Really) Work In Open-World Environments
summary: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) have been extensively used in robotics applications, achieving great success in various manipulation problems. More recently, VLAs have been used in long-horizon tasks and evaluated on benchmarks, such as BEHAVIOR1K (B1K), for solving complex household chores. The common metric for measuring progress in such benchmarks is success rate or partial score based on satisfaction of progress-agnostic criteria, meaning only the final states of the objects are considered, regardless of the events that lead to such states. In this paper, we argue that using such evaluation protocols say little about safety aspects of operation and can potentially exaggerate reported performance, undermining core challenges for future real-world deployment. To this end, we conduct a thorough analysis of state-of-the-art models on the B1K Challenge and evaluate policies in terms of robustness via reproducibility and consistency of performance, safety aspects of policies operations, task awareness, and key elements leading to the incompletion of tasks. We then propose evaluation protocols to capture safety violations to better measure the true performance of the policies in more complex and interactive scenarios. At the end, we discuss the limitations of the existing VLAs and motivate future research.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that current evaluation metrics for VLAs in robotics (e.g., success rate) overlook safety and exaggerate performance, then proposes new protocols to capture safety violations and better assess true policy robustness in complex tasks.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation protocols for VLAs in long-horizon robotics tasks rely on success rates based on final object states, ignoring safety aspects and potentially overstating performance, which undermines real-world deployment challenges.
AI.method: The authors conduct thorough analysis of state-of-the-art VLAs on the BEHAVIOR1K benchmark, evaluating policies across robustness (reproducibility & consistency), safety aspects, task awareness, and causes of task incompletion. They then propose new evaluation protocols to capture safety violations.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that existing success-rate metrics say little about safety, exaggerate reported performance, and fail to capture critical issues for real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: Current VLA evaluation protocols are insufficient for safe real-world deployment; new safety-aware metrics are needed, and existing VLAs have significant limitations that require further research.

title: A Hidden Markov Framework for Physically Interpretable Arc Stability Dynamics in Welding Systems
summary: Electric arc welding (EAW) exhibits strongly non stationary and temporally evolving behavior, making reliable assessment of arc stability difficult using conventional frame based approaches. In this study, arc dynamics are modeled as a sequence of latent operational regimes within a probabilistic state-space framework. The welding current signal is transformed into a time-frequency domain using Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT), and a set of physically meaningful spectral descriptors, including energy, entropy, and centroid, is extracted to construct the observation sequence. A Hidden Markov Model (HMM) is employed to capture temporal dependencies and estimate the evolution of arc states. The analysis reveals three dominant regimes, transient, stable, and extinction, with a clear monotonic increase in spectral energy and a corresponding decrease in entropy, indicating reduced variability under stable conditions. Despite partial overlap in the feature space, the inferred state sequence exhibits strong temporal coherence, supported by high state persistence and low transition rates. These findings highlight the limitations of static classification and emphasize the importance of temporal modeling. The proposed framework provides an interpretable and physically consistent representation of arc behavior, enabling more realistic monitoring and analysis of stability dynamics in welding processes.

AI.tldr: This paper models electric arc welding dynamics using Hidden Markov Models with spectral features from welding current, identifying three regimes (transient, stable, extinction) and demonstrating that temporal modeling outperforms static classification for arc stability assessment.
AI.motivation: Electric arc welding exhibits non-stationary, temporally evolving behavior that makes arc stability assessment difficult using conventional frame-based (static) approaches.
AI.method: Welding current is transformed via STFT into spectral descriptors (energy, entropy, centroid), then a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) is used to model temporal dependencies and estimate latent arc states.
AI.result: Three dominant regimes identified: transient, stable, and extinction. Stable regime shows monotonic increase in spectral energy and decrease in entropy. Inferred states exhibit strong temporal coherence with high persistence and low transition rates.
AI.conclusion: Temporal modeling (HMM) is superior to static classification for arc stability analysis. The framework provides an interpretable, physically consistent representation of arc behavior for realistic process monitoring.

title: Spatiotemporal 2-D Polar Codes over Non-Uniform MIMO Channels: A Reliability-Aware Construction Approach
summary: With the increasing demand for ultra-reliable and low-latency communication (URLLC), spatiotemporal two-dimensional (2-D) channel coding has received growing interest. By leveraging the spatial degrees of freedom in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems, it shortens the time-domain blocklength, thereby reducing latency and enhancing reliability. However, existing spatiotemporal coding schemes typically assume uniform reliability across spatial streams. This assumption does not hold in practical MIMO channels, where the underlying propagation environment generally leads to unequal spatial-eigenmode gains and reliabilities, making the conventional Gaussian-approximation-based construction for 2-D polar codes less effective. This paper investigates spatiotemporal 2-D polar coding over non-uniform MIMO channels, where the spatial domain exhibits inherently heterogeneous signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs). We propose a reciprocal channel approximation (RCA)-based reliability-aware 2-D polar coding framework that accurately characterizes such heterogeneous SNRs without relying on log-likelihood-ratio distribution assumptions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed RCA-based spatiotemporal 2-D polar coding scheme achieves clear performance gains and strong robustness, confirming its effectiveness in jointly exploiting temporal and spatial polarization for URLLC in practical MIMO systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a reciprocal channel approximation (RCA)-based reliability-aware 2-D polar coding framework for spatiotemporal coding over non-uniform MIMO channels with heterogeneous SNRs, achieving performance gains and robustness for URLLC.
AI.motivation: Existing spatiotemporal coding schemes assume uniform reliability across spatial streams, which does not hold in practical MIMO channels due to unequal spatial-eigenmode gains, making conventional Gaussian-approximation-based 2-D polar code construction less effective.
AI.method: The authors propose a reciprocal channel approximation (RCA)-based reliability-aware 2-D polar coding framework that accurately characterizes heterogeneous SNRs across spatial streams without relying on log-likelihood-ratio distribution assumptions.
AI.result: Simulation results show clear performance gains and strong robustness compared to conventional approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RCA-based spatiotemporal 2-D polar coding scheme effectively exploits temporal and spatial polarization for URLLC in practical MIMO systems with non-uniform channels.

title: Optimizing Diffusion Priors with a Single Observation
summary: While diffusion priors generate high-quality posterior samples across many inverse problems, they are often trained on limited training sets or purely simulated data, thus inheriting the errors and biases of these underlying sources. Current approaches to finetuning diffusion models rely on a large number of observations with varying forward operators, which can be difficult to collect for many applications, and thus lead to overfitting when the measurement set is small. We propose a method for tuning a prior from only a single observation by combining existing diffusion priors into a single product-of-experts prior and identifying the exponents that maximize the Bayesian evidence. We validate our method on real-world inverse problems, including black hole imaging, where the true prior is unknown a priori, and image deblurring with text-conditioned priors. We find that the evidence is often maximized by priors that extend beyond those trained on a single dataset. By generalizing the prior through exponent weighting, our approach enables posterior sampling from both tempered and combined diffusion models, yielding more flexible priors that improve the trustworthiness of the resulting posterior image distribution.

AI.tldr: Proposes a method to finetune diffusion priors from a single observation by combining multiple existing priors via product-of-experts and maximizing Bayesian evidence, validated on black hole imaging and deblurring tasks.
AI.motivation: Diffusion priors inherit errors/biases from limited/biased training data; existing finetuning approaches require many observations with varying forward operators, which is impractical for many applications.
AI.method: Combines existing diffusion priors into a product-of-experts prior, identifies optimal exponent weights by maximizing Bayesian evidence using only a single observation.
AI.result: Evidence is often maximized by priors extending beyond single-dataset training; enables posterior sampling from tempered and combined diffusion models with improved flexibility.
AI.conclusion: This single-observation tuning approach yields more flexible and trustworthy posterior image distributions, improving upon biased priors without requiring large measurement sets.

title: Validating a Deep Learning Algorithm to Identify Patients with Glaucoma using Systemic Electronic Health Records
summary: We evaluated whether a glaucoma risk assessment (GRA) model trained on All of Us national data can identify patients at high probability of glaucoma using only systemic electronic health records (EHR) at an independent institution. In this cross-sectional study, 20,636 Stanford patients seen from November 2013 to January 2024 were included (15% with glaucoma). A pretrained GRA model was fine-tuned on the Stanford cohort and tested on a held-out set using demographics, systemic diagnoses, medications, laboratory results, and physical examination measurements as inputs. The best model achieved AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657. Calibration was consistent with clinical risk: the highest prediction decile showed the greatest glaucoma diagnosis rate (65.7%) and treatment rate (57.0%). Performance improved with more trainable layers up to 15 and with additional data. An EHR-only GRA model may enable scalable and accessible pre-screening without specialized imaging.

AI.tldr: A glaucoma risk assessment model trained on national data and fine-tuned at Stanford achieved AUROC 0.883 using only systemic EHR data (no eye imaging), showing potential for scalable pre-screening.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether a glaucoma risk model using only systemic EHR data (demographics, diagnoses, medications, labs, vitals) can identify high-risk patients without requiring specialized eye imaging, enabling more accessible and scalable pre-screening.
AI.method: Cross-sectional study with 20,636 Stanford patients (15% glaucoma). A pretrained GRA model from All of Us data was fine-tuned on Stanford cohort using demographics, systemic diagnoses/medications, lab results, and physical exam measurements. Tested on held-out set.
AI.result: Best model achieved AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657. Highest prediction decile had 65.7% glaucoma diagnosis rate and 57.0% treatment rate. Performance improved with more trainable layers (up to 15) and more data.
AI.conclusion: An EHR-only GRA model can effectively identify high-probability glaucoma patients without specialized imaging, enabling scalable, accessible pre-screening across healthcare settings.

title: HypEHR: Hyperbolic Modeling of Electronic Health Records for Efficient Question Answering
summary: Electronic health record (EHR) question answering is often handled by LLM-based pipelines that are costly to deploy and do not explicitly leverage the hierarchical structure of clinical data. Motivated by evidence that medical ontologies and patient trajectories exhibit hyperbolic geometry, we propose HypEHR, a compact Lorentzian model that embeds codes, visits, and questions in hyperbolic space and answers queries via geometry-consistent cross-attention with type-specific pointer heads. HypEHR is pretrained with next-visit diagnosis prediction and hierarchy-aware regularization to align representations with the ICD ontology. On two MIMIC-IV-based EHR-QA benchmarks, HypEHR approaches LLM-based methods while using far fewer parameters. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yuyuliu11037/HypEHR.

AI.tldr: HypEHR is a compact hyperbolic embedding model for EHR question answering that leverages clinical data's hierarchical structure, pretrains with ontology-aware objectives, and approaches LLM performance with far fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: LLM-based EHR QA pipelines are costly and fail to exploit the hierarchical structure of clinical data. Medical ontologies and patient trajectories exhibit hyperbolic geometry, motivating a more efficient geometry-aware approach.
AI.method: HypEHR uses a Lorentzian (hyperbolic) model to embed codes, visits, and questions, performs geometry-consistent cross-attention with type-specific pointer heads, and pretrains with next-visit diagnosis prediction and hierarchy-aware regularization aligned to the ICD ontology.
AI.result: On two MIMIC-IV-based EHR-QA benchmarks, HypEHR approaches the performance of LLM-based methods while using significantly fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic embedding is a promising, parameter-efficient alternative to LLMs for structured EHR question answering, and the code is publicly available.

title: CorridorVLA: Explicit Spatial Constraints for Generative Action Heads via Sparse Anchors
summary: Vision--Language--Action (VLA) models often use intermediate representations to connect multimodal inputs with continuous control, yet spatial guidance is often injected implicitly through latent features. We propose $CorridorVLA$, which predicts sparse spatial anchors as incremental physical changes (e.g., $Δ$-positions) and uses them to impose an explicit tolerance region in the training objective for action generation. The anchors define a corridor that guides a flow-matching action head: trajectories whose implied spatial evolution falls outside it receive corrective gradients, while minor deviations from contacts and execution noise are permitted. On the more challenging LIBERO-Plus benchmark, CorridorVLA yields consistent gains across both SmolVLA and GR00T, improving success rate by $3.4\%$--$12.4\%$ over the corresponding baselines; notably, our GR00T-Corr variant reaches a success rate of $83.21\%$. These results indicate that action-aligned physical cues can provide direct and interpretable constraints for generative action policies, complementing spatial guidance encoded in visual or latent forms. Code is available at https://github.com/corridorVLA.

AI.tldr: CorridorVLA improves VLA models by predicting sparse spatial anchors (Δ-positions) to define a tolerance corridor that guides flow-matching action generation, achieving significant gains on LIBERO-Plus (up to 83.21% success rate).
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models inject spatial guidance implicitly through latent features; the authors aim to provide explicit, interpretable spatial constraints for action generation.
AI.method: Predicts sparse spatial anchors as incremental physical changes, defines a tolerance corridor in the training objective, and uses a flow-matching action head that applies corrective gradients when trajectories deviate from the corridor.
AI.result: On LIBERO-Plus, improves success rate by 3.4%-12.4% over SmolVLA and GR00T baselines; GR00T-Corr variant achieves 83.21% success rate.
AI.conclusion: Action-aligned physical cues provide direct, interpretable constraints for generative action policies, complementing spatial guidance encoded in visual or latent forms.

title: Dilated CNNs for Periodic Signal Processing: A Low-Complexity Approach
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures, the use of deep learning in IoT devices
summary: Denoising of periodic signals and accurate waveform estimation are core tasks across many signal processing domains, including speech, music, medical diagnostics, radio, and sonar. Although deep learning methods have recently shown performance improvements over classical approaches, they require substantial computational resources and are usually trained separately for each signal observation. This study proposes a computationally efficient method based on DCNN and Re-sampling, termed R-DCNN, designed for operation under strict power and resource constraints. The approach targets signals with varying fundamental frequencies and requires only a single observation for training. It generalizes to additional signals via a lightweight resampling step that aligns time scales in signals with different frequencies to re-use the same network weights. Despite its low computational complexity, R-DCNN achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art classical methods, such as autoregressive (AR)-based techniques, as well as conventional DCNNs trained individually for each observation. This combination of efficiency and performance makes the proposed method particularly well suited for deployment in resource-constrained environments without sacrificing denoising or estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: Proposes R-DCNN, a lightweight deep learning method combining DCNN with resampling for periodic signal denoising and waveform estimation, achieving state-of-the-art performance with minimal computational cost and single-observation training.
AI.motivation: Deep learning methods for signal denoising achieve high performance but require substantial computational resources and separate training per signal observation. The goal is to develop an efficient method suitable for resource-constrained environments.
AI.method: R-DCNN combines a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) with a lightweight resampling step that aligns time scales of signals with varying fundamental frequencies, enabling a single network to generalize across signals without retraining.
AI.result: R-DCNN achieves denoising performance comparable to state-of-the-art classical methods (e.g., AR-based) and standard DCNNs trained individually per observation, while requiring significantly lower computational resources.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is well-suited for resource-constrained deployment, offering an efficient balance between computational complexity and denoising/estimation accuracy.

title: Hierarchical Joint Source-Channel Coding with Constrained Information Leakage
summary: This paper studies the hierarchical joint source-channel coding with information leakage constraint in the first-phase reconstruction and distortion constraints. The receiver's access to the data varies and is evaluated by the quality of the side information. Due to the consideration of channel capacity limitation or the efficiency of the system performance, the encoder may send some additional information in Phase 1 that can only be decoded in Phase 2 with higher-quality side information. While this can optimize the overall performance, the additional information causes excessive information leakage. We provide general inner and outer bounds for the conditions such that a given distortion-leakage pair $(D_1,D_2,L)$ is achievable, together with a capacity-achieving condition.

AI.tldr: This paper studies hierarchical joint source-channel coding with information leakage constraints, where some data sent in Phase 1 can only be decoded in Phase 2 with higher-quality side information. This improves performance but causes extra leakage. The paper provides inner and outer bounds for achievable distortion-leakage pairs.
AI.motivation: To balance the trade-off between system performance (distortion) and information leakage in hierarchical communication systems where receivers have varying side information quality. Sending additional information in Phase 1 that is only decodable in Phase 2 can improve performance but creates security risks.
AI.method: The paper develops general inner and outer bounds for conditions under which a given distortion-leakage pair (D1, D2, L) is achievable, along with a capacity-achieving condition. It uses hierarchical joint source-channel coding with information leakage constraints.
AI.result: The authors provide general inner and outer bounds for the conditions such that a given distortion-leakage pair (D1, D2, L) is achievable, together with a capacity-achieving condition.
AI.conclusion: The study offers theoretical bounds for achieving specific distortion-leakage trade-offs in hierarchical joint source-channel coding, highlighting the optimization of overall performance while managing excessive information leakage from additional Phase 1 transmissions.

title: Foveated Reasoning: Stateful, Action-based Visual Focusing for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models benefit from high-resolution images, but the increase in visual-token count incurs high compute overhead. Humans resolve this tension via foveation: a coarse view guides "where to look", while selectively acquired high-acuity evidence refines "what to think". We introduce Foveated Reasoner, an autoregressive vision-language framework that unifies foveation and reasoning within a single decoding trajectory. Starting from a low-resolution view, the model triggers foveation only when needed, retrieves high-resolution evidence from selected regions, and injects it back into the same decoding trajectory. We train the method with a two-stage pipeline: coldstart supervision to bootstrap foveation behavior, followed by reinforcement learning to jointly improve evidence acquisition and task accuracy while discouraging trivial "see-everything" solutions. Experiments show that the method learns effective foveation policies and achieves stronger accuracy under tight visual-token budgets across multiple vision-language benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Foveated Reasoner is an autoregressive vision-language model that mimics human foveation—using low-resolution views to guide where to look, then selectively acquiring high-resolution evidence—to reduce visual-token overhead while maintaining task accuracy, trained via coldstart supervision and reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: To resolve the tension between the benefits of high-resolution images and the high compute cost from increased visual-token count, by mimicking human foveation (coarse-to-fine visual attention).
AI.method: An autoregressive framework that unifies foveation and reasoning in a single decoding trajectory: starts with low-resolution view, triggers foveation when needed, retrieves high-resolution evidence from selected regions, and injects it back into the same decoding trajectory. Trained with two-stage pipeline: (1) coldstart supervision to bootstrap foveation, (2) reinforcement learning to jointly improve evidence acquisition and task accuracy while discouraging trivial "see-everything" behavior.
AI.result: The method learns effective foveation policies and achieves stronger accuracy under tight visual-token budgets across multiple vision-language benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Foveated Reasoner demonstrates that selectively acquiring high-resolution evidence (foveation) can reduce compute overhead while improving or maintaining accuracy, offering a more efficient alternative to always using full-resolution images in vision-language models.

title: ILDR: Geometric Early Detection of Grokking
summary: Grokking describes a delayed generalization phenomenon in which a neural network achieves perfect training accuracy long before validation accuracy improves, followed by an abrupt transition to strong generalization. Existing detection signals are indirect: weight norm reflects parameter-space regularization and consistently lags the transition, while GrokFast's slow gradient EMA, used without gradient amplification, is unstable across seeds with standard deviation exceeding mean lead time. We propose the Inter/Intra-class Distance Ratio (ILDR), a geometric metric computed on second-to-last layer representations as the ratio of inter-class centroid separation to intra-class scatter. ILDR provides an early detection signal: it rises and crosses a threshold at 2.5 times its baseline before the grokking transition appears in validation accuracy, indicating early geometric reorganization in representation space. Grounded in Fisher's linear discriminant criterion, ILDR requires no eigendecomposition and runs in O(|C|^2 + N). It is evaluated exclusively on held-out data, making it robust to memorization effects. Across modular arithmetic and permutation group composition (S5), ILDR leads the grokking transition by 9 to 73 percent of the training budget, with lead time increasing with task algebraic complexity. Over eight random seeds, ILDR leads by 950 +/- 250 steps with a coefficient of variation of 26 percent, and post-grokking variance drops by 1696 times, consistent with a sharp phase transition in representation space. Using ILDR as an early stopping trigger reduces training by 18.6 percent on average. Optimizer interventions triggered at the ILDR threshold demonstrate bidirectional control over the transition, suggesting ILDR tracks representational conditions underlying generalization rather than a downstream correlate.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes ILDR (Inter/Intra-class Distance Ratio), a geometric metric that detects the grokking phenomenon earlier than existing methods by measuring the ratio of inter-class centroid separation to intra-class scatter in representation space, enabling early stopping and bidirectional control of the generalization transition.
AI.motivation: Existing grokking detection signals (weight norm, GrokFast's gradient EMA) are either lagging or unstable. The authors aim to find a reliable, early detection signal that captures geometric reorganization before the validation accuracy transition.
AI.method: Proposes ILDR (Inter/Intra-class Distance Ratio), a Fisher's linear discriminant-inspired metric computed on second-to-last layer representations as the ratio of inter-class centroid separation to intra-class scatter. ILDR is evaluated on held-out data, requires no eigendecomposition, and runs in O(|C|^2 + N).
