Applications show that compact datasets can be made linearly separable by width-n or width-(n+1) deep neural networks with specific activation functions.
AI.motivation: To establish a mathematical theory for relocating compact sets via self-diffeomorphisms, motivated by potential applications in data classification and making datasets linearly separable using deep neural networks.
AI.method: The authors develop a diffeomorphism-based relocation theory for compact sets in ℝ^n, then prove an embedding theorem into ℝ^{n+1} for linear separability. They apply this theory to DNNs with Leaky-ReLU, ELU, or SELU activations.
AI.result: Finite compact datasets in ℝ^n can be made linearly separable by width-n DNNs (under mild conditions) or width-(n+1) DNNs (for mutually disjoint compact sets) with specific activation functions.
AI.conclusion: The established theory bridges geometric relocation and neural network classification, providing theoretical guarantees for linear separability of finite compact datasets via DNNs of controlled width.

title: Enabling and Inhibitory Pathways of University Students' Willingness to Disclose AI Use: A Cognition-Affect-Conation Perspective
summary: The increasing integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education has raised important questions regarding students' transparency in reporting AI-assisted work. This study investigates the psychological mechanisms underlying university students' willingness to disclose AI use by applying the Cognition--Affect--Conation (CAC) framework. A sequential explanatory mixed-methods design was employed. In the quantitative phase, survey data were collected from 546 university students and analysed using structural equation modelling to examine the relationships among cognitive perceptions, affective responses, and disclosure intention. In the qualitative phase, semi-structured interviews with 22 students were conducted to further interpret the quantitative findings. The results indicate that psychological safety significantly increases students' willingness to disclose AI use and is positively shaped by perceived fairness, perceived teacher support, and perceived organisational support. Conversely, evaluation apprehension reduces disclosure intention and psychological safety, and is strengthened by perceived stigma, perceived uncertainty, and privacy concern. Qualitative findings further reveal that institutional clarity and supportive instructional practices encourage openness, whereas policy ambiguity and fear of negative evaluation often lead students to adopt cautious or strategic disclosure practices. Overall, the study highlights the dual role of enabling and inhibitory psychological mechanisms in shaping AI-use disclosure and underscores the importance of supportive institutional environments and clear guidance for promoting responsible AI transparency in higher education.

AI.tldr: This study examines university students' willingness to disclose AI use through the CAC framework, finding that psychological safety promotes disclosure while evaluation apprehension inhibits it, with institutional clarity and supportive practices playing key roles.
AI.motivation: To understand the psychological mechanisms behind students' transparency in reporting AI-assisted work in higher education, addressing growing concerns about AI use disclosure.
AI.method: Sequential explanatory mixed-methods design: quantitative phase (survey of 546 students, structural equation modeling) followed by qualitative phase (semi-structured interviews with 22 students).
AI.result: Psychological safety increases willingness to disclose AI use (shaped by perceived fairness, teacher support, and organizational support). Evaluation apprehension reduces both disclosure intention and psychological safety (strengthened by perceived stigma, uncertainty, and privacy concern). Qualitative findings highlight institutional clarity and supportive practices as enablers, while policy ambiguity and fear of negative evaluation lead to cautious/strategic disclosure.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals dual enabling and inhibitory psychological mechanisms in AI-use disclosure, emphasizing the need for supportive institutional environments and clear guidance to promote responsible AI transparency in higher education.

title: Rethinking Cross-Domain Evaluation for Face Forgery Detection with Semantic Fine-grained Alignment and Mixture-of-Experts
comment: The source code is available at https://github.com/Yuhan-Luo/Semantic-Fine-grained-Alignment-and-Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Nowadays, visual data forgery detection plays an increasingly important role in social and economic security with the rapid development of generative models. Existing face forgery detectors still can't achieve satisfactory performance because of poor generalization ability across datasets. The key factor that led to this phenomenon is the lack of suitable metrics: the commonly used cross-dataset AUC metric fails to reveal an important issue where detection scores may shift significantly across data domains. To explicitly evaluate cross-domain score comparability, we propose \textbf{Cross-AUC}, an evaluation metric that can compute AUC across dataset pairs by contrasting real samples from one dataset with fake samples from another (and vice versa). It is interesting to find that evaluating representative detectors under the Cross-AUC metric reveals substantial performance drops, exposing an overlooked robustness problem. Besides, we also propose the novel framework \textbf{S}emantic \textbf{F}ine-grained \textbf{A}lignment and \textbf{M}ixture-of-Experts (\textbf{SFAM}), consisting of a patch-level image-text alignment module that enhances CLIP's sensitivity to manipulation artifacts, and the facial region mixture-of-experts module, which routes features from different facial regions to specialized experts for region-aware forgery analysis. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments on the public datasets prove that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared with the state-of-the-art methods with various suitable metrics.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Cross-AUC, a new evaluation metric for cross-domain score comparability in face forgery detection, and SFAM, a novel framework combining patch-level image-text alignment with facial region mixture-of-experts for improved detection performance and generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing face forgery detectors suffer from poor cross-dataset generalization because the commonly used cross-dataset AUC metric fails to reveal detection score shifts across different data domains.
AI.method: (1) Cross-AUC metric: computes AUC across dataset pairs by contrasting real samples from one dataset with fake samples from another (and vice versa). (2) SFAM framework: patch-level image-text alignment module enhancing CLIP's sensitivity to manipulation artifacts, plus facial region mixture-of-experts module for region-aware forgery analysis.
AI.result: Cross-AUC reveals substantial performance drops in existing detectors that were previously overlooked. SFAM achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods across various metrics on public datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Cross-AUC metric and SFAM framework effectively address the generalization problem in face forgery detection, providing both better evaluation and improved detection capabilities.

title: Supervised Learning Has a Necessary Geometric Blind Spot: Theory, Consequences, and Minimal Repair
comment: 29 pages. Code: https://github.com/vishalstark512/PMH. Preprint, not peer-reviewed. Affiliation: KU Leuven, Belgium
summary:
We prove that empirical risk minimisation (ERM) imposes a necessary geometric constraint on learned representations: any encoder that minimises supervised loss must retain non-zero Jacobian sensitivity in directions that are label-correlated in training data but nuisance at test time. This is not a contingent failure of current methods; it is a mathematical consequence of the supervised objective itself. We call this the geometric blind spot of supervised learning (Theorem 1), and show it holds across proper scoring rules, architectures, and dataset sizes.
  This single theorem unifies four lines of prior empirical work that were previously treated separately: non-robust predictive features, texture bias, corruption fragility, and the robustness-accuracy tradeoff. In this framing, adversarial vulnerability is one consequence of a broader structural fact about supervised learning geometry.
  We introduce Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI), a diagnostic that measures the theorem's bounded quantity directly, and show why common alternatives miss the key failure mode. PGD adversarial training reaches Jacobian Frobenius 2.91 yet has the worst clean-input geometry (TDI 1.336), while PMH achieves TDI 0.904. TDI is the only metric that detects this dissociation because it measures isotropic path-length distortion -- the exact quantity Theorem 1 bounds.
  Across seven vision tasks, BERT/SST-2, and ImageNet ViT-B/16 backbones used by CLIP, DINO, and SAM, the blind spot is measurable and repairable. It is present at foundation-model scale, worsens monotonically across language-model sizes (blind-spot ratio 0.860 to 0.765 to 0.742 from 66M to 340M), and is amplified by task-specific ERM fine-tuning (+54%), while PMH repairs it by 11x with one additional training term whose Gaussian form Proposition 5 proves is the unique perturbation law that uniformly penalises the encoder Jacobian.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that supervised ERM inherently forces learned representations to retain sensitivity to label-correlated but nuisance directions (a "geometric blind spot"), unifying four previously separate empirical phenomena. It introduces TDI as a diagnostic and shows PMH can repair this blind spot.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that the geometric blind spot in supervised learning is not an empirical failure but a mathematical necessity of the ERM objective itself, and to explain adversarial vulnerability, texture bias, corruption fragility, and the robustness-accuracy tradeoff as manifestations of the same underlying geometric constraint.
AI.method: The authors prove Theorem 1 establishing the geometric blind spot, introduce the Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI) to measure isotropic path-length distortion, and propose PMH (a Gaussian-form perturbation) as a repair method (Proposition 5). Experiments span vision tasks, BERT/SST-2, and ImageNet ViT-B/16 backbones (CLIP, DINO, SAM).
AI.result: PGD adversarial training achieves Jacobian Frobenius 2.91 but has worst TDI (1.336), while PMH achieves TDI 0.904. The blind spot worsens across language model sizes (0.860→0.765→0.742) and is amplified by ERM fine-tuning (+54%), while PMH repairs it by 11x.
AI.conclusion: The geometric blind spot is a fundamental structural fact about supervised learning, measurable across scales (including foundation models), and repairable via the proposed PMH method.

title: Bridging the Training-Deployment Gap: Gated Encoding and Multi-Scale Refinement for Efficient Quantization-Aware Image Enhancement
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures. Accepted at the Mobile AI (MAI) 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Image enhancement models for mobile devices often struggle to balance high output quality with the fast processing speeds required by mobile hardware. While recent deep learning models can enhance low-quality mobile photos into high-quality images, their performance is often degraded when converted to lower-precision formats for actual use on mobile phones. To address this training-deployment mismatch, we propose an efficient image enhancement model designed specifically for mobile deployment. Our approach uses a hierarchical network architecture with gated encoder blocks and multiscale refinement to preserve fine-grained visual features. Moreover, we incorporate Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to simulate the effects of low-precision representation during the training process. This allows the network to adapt and prevents the typical drop in quality seen with standard post-training quantization (PTQ). Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method produces high-fidelity visual output while maintaining the low computational overhead needed for practical use on standard mobile devices. The code will be available at https://github.com/GenAI4E/QATIE.git.

AI.tldr: A mobile-friendly image enhancement model using hierarchical architecture and Quantization-Aware Training to prevent quality loss when converting to low-precision formats for on-device deployment.
AI.motivation: Mobile image enhancement models face a training-deployment mismatch: models trained in high precision suffer quality degradation when converted to lower-precision formats required for practical mobile use.
AI.method: (1) Hierarchical network with gated encoder blocks and multiscale refinement for preserving fine visual features; (2) Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to simulate low-precision effects during training, adapting the network and avoiding quality drops seen with standard post-training quantization (PTQ).
AI.result: Produces high-fidelity visual output with low computational overhead suitable for standard mobile devices.
AI.conclusion: QAT combined with efficient architecture effectively bridges the training-deployment gap, enabling high-quality image enhancement on mobile hardware without performance degradation.

title: Frozen LLMs as Map-Aware Spatio-Temporal Reasoners for Vehicle Trajectory Prediction
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated strong reasoning capabilities and attracted increasing research attention in the field of autonomous driving (AD). However, safe application of LLMs on AD perception and prediction still requires a thorough understanding of both the dynamic traffic agents and the static road infrastructure. To this end, this study introduces a framework to evaluate the capability of LLMs in understanding the behaviors of dynamic traffic agents and the topology of road networks. The framework leverages frozen LLMs as the reasoning engine, employing a traffic encoder to extract spatial-level scene features from observed trajectories of agents, while a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) encodes the local high-definition (HD) maps. To assess the intrinsic reasoning ability of LLMs, the extracted scene features are then transformed into LLM-compatible tokens via a reprogramming adapter. By residing the prediction burden with the LLMs, a simpler linear decoder is applied to output future trajectories. The framework enables a quantitative analysis of the influence of multi-modal information, especially the impact of map semantics on trajectory prediction accuracy, and allows seamless integration of frozen LLMs with minimal adaptation, thereby demonstrating strong generalizability across diverse LLM architectures and providing a unified platform for model evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework that uses frozen LLMs as reasoning engines for autonomous driving trajectory prediction, integrating traffic encoders and CNN-based HD map encoders, with a reprogramming adapter to convert scene features into LLM-compatible tokens, enabling quantitative analysis of multi-modal information's impact on prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: To evaluate LLMs' capability in understanding dynamic traffic agents and road topology for safe autonomous driving perception and prediction.
AI.method: The framework uses frozen LLMs as reasoning engines with a traffic encoder for spatial-level scene features, a CNN for HD map encoding, and a reprogramming adapter to convert features into LLM-compatible tokens. A linear decoder outputs future trajectories.
AI.result: The framework enables quantitative analysis of multi-modal information impact on trajectory prediction accuracy and demonstrates strong generalizability across diverse LLM architectures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a unified platform for model evaluation with minimal adaptation, showing that frozen LLMs can be effectively integrated with multi-modal traffic and map information for trajectory prediction.

title: Even More Guarantees for Variational Inference in the Presence of Symmetries
comment: Accepted for presentation at the OPTIMAL Workshop at AISTATS 2026
summary: When approximating an intractable density via variational inference (VI) the variational family is typically chosen as a simple parametric family that very likely does not contain the target. This raises the question: Under which conditions can we recover characteristics of the target despite misspecification? In this work, we extend previous results on robust VI with location-scale families under target symmetries. We derive sufficient conditions guaranteeing exact recovery of the mean when using the forward Kullback-Leibler divergence and $α$-divergences. We further show how and why optimization can fail to recover the target mean in the absence of our sufficient conditions, providing initial guidelines on the choice of the variational family and $α$-value.

AI.tldr: This paper studies when variational inference can recover the true mean of a target distribution even when the variational family is misspecified (doesn't contain the target), deriving sufficient conditions for exact mean recovery using forward KL and α-divergences under target symmetries.
AI.motivation: To understand when characteristics of an intractable target density (specifically the mean) can be recovered via variational inference despite using a misspecified variational family that likely doesn't contain the true target.
AI.method: Extends prior work on robust VI with location-scale families under target symmetries; derives sufficient conditions for exact mean recovery using forward KL divergence and α-divergences; analyzes failure cases when conditions are not met.
AI.result: Identifies sufficient conditions guaranteeing exact recovery of the target mean under forward KL and α-divergences; shows how optimization can fail in the absence of these conditions.
AI.conclusion: Provides initial guidelines for choosing variational families and α-values to ensure robust mean recovery despite model misspecification.

title: Thinking with Reasoning Skills: Fewer Tokens, More Accuracy
comment: 10 pages, The 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics -- Industry Track
summary: Reasoning LLMs often spend substantial tokens on long intermediate reasoning traces (e.g., chain-of-thought) when solving new problems. We propose to summarize and store reusable reasoning skills distilled from extensive deliberation and trial-and-error exploration, and to retrieve these skills at inference time to guide future reasoning. Unlike the prevailing \emph{reasoning from scratch} paradigm, our approach first recalls relevant skills for each query, helping the model avoid redundant detours and focus on effective solution paths. We evaluate our method on coding and mathematical reasoning tasks, and find that it significantly reduces reasoning tokens while improving overall performance. The resulting lower per-request cost indicates strong practical and economic potential for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a skill-based reasoning approach that summarizes and stores reusable reasoning skills from prior deliberation, then retrieves them at inference time to reduce token usage and improve performance on coding and math tasks.
AI.motivation: Current reasoning LLMs waste substantial tokens on long intermediate reasoning traces (e.g., chain-of-thought) when solving new problems, leading to high computational cost and redundant exploration.
AI.method: The method summarizes and stores reusable reasoning skills distilled from extensive deliberation and trial-and-error exploration. At inference time, relevant skills are retrieved for each query to guide the model's reasoning, avoiding redundant detours.
AI.result: The approach significantly reduces reasoning tokens while improving overall performance on coding and mathematical reasoning tasks, leading to lower per-request cost.
AI.conclusion: The proposed skill-based reasoning paradigm shows strong practical and economic potential for real-world deployment by reducing inference costs while maintaining or improving quality.

title: VFM$^{4}$SDG: Unveiling the Power of VFMs for Single-Domain Generalized Object Detection
summary: In real-world scenarios, continual changes in weather, illumination, and imaging conditions cause significant domain shifts, leading detectors trained on a single source domain to degrade severely in unseen environments. Existing single-domain generalized object detection (SDGOD) methods mainly rely on data augmentation or domain-invariant representation learning, but pay limited attention to detector mechanisms, leaving clear limitations under complex domain shifts. Through analytical experiments, we find that performance degradation is dominated by increasing missed detections, which fundamentally arises from reduced cross-domain stability of the detector: object-background and inter-instance relations become less stable in the encoding stage, while semantic-spatial alignment of query representations also becomes harder to maintain in the decoding stage. To this end, we propose VFM$^{4}$SDG, a dual-prior learning framework for SDGOD, which introduces a frozen vision foundation model (VFM) as a transferable cross-domain stability prior into detector representation learning and query modeling. In the encoding stage, we propose Cross-domain Stable Relational Prior Distillation to enhance the robustness of object-background and inter-instance relational modeling. In the decoding stage, we propose Semantic-Contextual Prior-based Query Enhancement, which injects category-level semantic prototypes and global visual context into queries to improve their semantic recognition and spatial localization stability in unseen domains. Extensive experiments show that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing SOTA methods on standard SDGOD benchmarks and two mainstream DETR-based detectors, demonstrating its effectiveness, robustness, and generality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes VFM$^{4}$SDG, a dual-prior learning framework that uses a frozen vision foundation model to improve cross-domain stability in object detection under domain shifts, addressing missed detections caused by unstable object-background relations and query alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing single-domain generalized object detection (SDGOD) methods rely on data augmentation or domain-invariant representation learning but pay limited attention to detector mechanisms, leaving clear limitations under complex domain shifts. Performance degradation is dominated by increasing missed detections due to reduced cross-domain stability.
AI.method: VFM$^{4}$SDG introduces a frozen vision foundation model (VFM) as a transferable cross-domain stability prior. It has two components: (1) Cross-domain Stable Relational Prior Distillation in the encoding stage to enhance object-background and inter-instance relational modeling; (2) Semantic-Contextual Prior-based Query Enhancement in the decoding stage to inject category-level semantic prototypes and global visual context into queries.
AI.result: The proposed method consistently outperforms existing SOTA methods on standard SDGOD benchmarks and two mainstream DETR-based detectors, demonstrating effectiveness, robustness, and generality.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging frozen VFMs as cross-domain stability priors, combined with relational distillation and semantic-contextual query enhancement, is an effective and generalizable approach for single-domain generalized object detection under complex domain shifts.

title: A Green-Integral-Constrained Neural Solver with Stochastic Physics-Informed Regularization
summary: Standard physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) struggle to simulate highly oscillatory Helmholtz solutions in heterogeneous media because pointwise minimization of second-order PDE residuals is computationally expensive, biased toward smooth solutions, and requires artificial absorbing boundary layers to restrict the solution. To overcome these challenges, we introduce a Green-Integral (GI) neural solver for the acoustic Helmholtz equation. It departs from the PDE-residual-based formulation by enforcing wave physics through an integral representation that imposes a nonlocal constraint. Oscillatory behavior and outgoing radiation are encoded directly through the integral kernel, eliminating second-order spatial derivatives and enforcing physical solutions without additional boundary layers. Theoretically, optimizing this GI loss via a neural network acts as a spectrally tuned preconditioned iteration, enabling convergence in heterogeneous media where the classical Born series diverges. By exploiting FFT-based convolution to accelerate the GI loss evaluation, our approach substantially reduces GPU memory usage and training time. However, this efficiency relies on a fixed regular grid, which can limit local resolution. To improve local accuracy in strong scattering regions, we also propose a hybrid GI+PDE loss, enforcing a lightweight Helmholtz residual at a small number of nonuniformly sampled collocation points. We evaluate our method on seismic benchmark models characterized by structural contrasts and subwavelength heterogeneity at frequencies up to 20Hz. GI-based training consistently outperforms PDE-based PINNs, reducing computational cost by over a factor of ten. In models with localized scattering, the hybrid loss yields the most accurate reconstructions, providing a stable, efficient, and physically grounded alternative.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Green-Integral (GI) neural solver for the acoustic Helmholtz equation, replacing standard PDE-residual PINNs with an integral formulation that eliminates second-order derivatives, enforces outgoing radiation without boundary layers, and uses FFT-based convolution for efficiency. The method outperforms PDE-based PINNs by over 10x in computational cost, and a hybrid GI+PDE loss further improves accuracy in strong scattering regions.
AI.motivation: Standard PINNs struggle with highly oscillatory Helmholtz solutions in heterogeneous media due to: (1) expensive pointwise minimization of second-order PDE residuals, (2) bias toward smooth solutions, and (3) the need for artificial absorbing boundary layers. The authors aim to overcome these limitations with a more physically grounded integral formulation.
AI.method: The GI neural solver enforces wave physics through an integral representation (Green's integral) that imposes a nonlocal constraint, encoding oscillatory behavior and outgoing radiation directly via the integral kernel. This eliminates second-order spatial derivatives and absorbing boundary layers. The GI loss optimization acts as a spectrally tuned preconditioned iteration (converging where Born series diverges). FFT-based convolution accelerates loss evaluation, and a hybrid GI+PDE loss improves local accuracy using nonuniform collocation points.
AI.result: On seismic benchmark models with structural contrasts and subwavelength heterogeneity up to 20Hz, GI-based training consistently outperforms PDE-based PINNs, reducing computational cost by over 10x. The hybrid loss yields the most accurate reconstructions in models with localized scattering.
AI.conclusion: The GI neural solver provides a stable, efficient, and physically grounded alternative to standard PINNs for simulating wave propagation in heterogeneous media, particularly for highly oscillatory Helmholtz problems.

title: Who Defines "Best"? Towards Interactive, User-Defined Evaluation of LLM Leaderboards
comment: Accepted to the 2026 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT 2026)
summary: LLM leaderboards are widely used to compare models and guide deployment decisions. However, leaderboard rankings are shaped by evaluation priorities set by benchmark designers, rather than by the diverse goals and constraints of actual users and organizations. A single aggregate score often obscures how models behave across different prompt types and compositions. In this work, we conduct an in-depth analysis of the dataset used in the LMArena (formerly Chatbot Arena) benchmark and investigate this evaluation challenge by designing an interactive visualization interface as a design probe. Our analysis reveals that the dataset is heavily skewed toward certain topics, that model rankings vary across prompt slices, and that preference-based judgments are used in ways that blur their intended scope. Building on this analysis, we introduce a visualization interface that allows users to define their own evaluation priorities by selecting and weighting prompt slices and to explore how rankings change accordingly. A qualitative study suggests that this interactive approach improves transparency and supports more context-specific model evaluation, pointing toward alternative ways to design and use LLM leaderboards.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the LMArena benchmark, finds it's skewed toward certain topics, and proposes an interactive visualization interface that lets users define their own evaluation priorities for more transparent and context-specific LLM comparison.
AI.motivation: LLM leaderboards use aggregate scores that obscure model behavior across different prompt types, and rankings are shaped by benchmark designers' priorities rather than diverse user needs.
AI.method: In-depth analysis of LMArena dataset revealing topic skew and ranking variation; design of an interactive visualization interface as a design probe; qualitative study to evaluate the interface.
AI.result: The LMArena dataset is heavily skewed toward certain topics; model rankings vary significantly across different prompt slices; preference-based judgments blur their intended scope. The interactive visualization improved transparency and supported context-specific evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Interactive, user-driven evaluation interfaces can complement traditional leaderboards by allowing users to define their own priorities, pointing toward alternative ways to design LLM leaderboards.

title: Gmd: Gaussian mixture descriptor for pair matching of 3D fragments
comment: 24 pages, 10 figures. Published in Multimedia Systems
summary: In the automatic reassembly of fragments acquired using laser scanners to reconstruct objects, a crucial step is the matching of fractured surfaces. In this paper, we propose a novel local descriptor that uses the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to fit the distribution of points, allowing for the description and matching of fractured surfaces of fragments. Our method involves dividing a local surface patch into concave and convex regions for estimating the k value of GMM. Then the final Gaussian Mixture Descriptor (GMD) of the fractured surface is formed by merging the regional GMDs. To measure the similarities between GMDs for determining adjacent fragments, we employ the L2 distance and align the fragments using Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) and Iterative Closest Point (ICP). The extensive experiments on real-scanned public datasets and Terracotta datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach; furthermore, the comparisons with several existing methods also validate the advantage of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)-based local descriptor (GMD) for matching fractured surfaces in 3D fragment reassembly, validated on real-scanned and Terracotta datasets.
AI.motivation: To improve the accuracy of matching fractured surfaces in automatic reassembly of laser-scanned fragments, which is a crucial step for reconstructing objects.
AI.method: 1) Divides local surface patches into concave and convex regions; 2) Estimates k value of GMM for each region; 3) Forms a Gaussian Mixture Descriptor (GMD) by merging regional GMDs; 4) Measures similarity using L2 distance; 5) Aligns fragments using RANSAC and ICP.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-scanned public datasets and Terracotta datasets show effectiveness; comparisons with existing methods demonstrate advantages.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GMD descriptor is effective for fractured surface matching in 3D fragment reassembly, outperforming several existing methods.

title: Inferring High-Level Events from Timestamped Data: Complexity and Medical Applications
comment: This is the full version (with appendix) of a paper appearing at the 23rd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2026)
summary: In this paper, we develop a novel logic-based approach to detecting high-level temporally extended events from timestamped data and background knowledge. Our framework employs logical rules to capture existence and termination conditions for simple temporal events and to combine these into meta-events. In the medical domain, for example, disease episodes and therapies are inferred from timestamped clinical observations, such as diagnoses and drug administrations stored in patient records, and can be further combined into higher-level disease events. As some incorrect events might be inferred, we use constraints to identify incompatible combinations of events and propose a repair mechanism to select preferred consistent sets of events. While reasoning in the full framework is intractable, we identify relevant restrictions that ensure polynomial-time data complexity. Our prototype system implements core components of the approach using answer set programming. An evaluation on a lung cancer use case supports the interest of the approach, both in terms of computational feasibility and positive alignment of our results with medical expert opinions. While strongly motivated by the needs of the healthcare domain, our framework is purposely generic, enabling its reuse in other areas.

AI.tldr: A generic logic-based framework for detecting high-level temporal events from timestamped data, with constraint-based repair for consistency, demonstrated on medical data using answer set programming.
AI.motivation: To enable automated detection of complex, temporally extended events (e.g., disease episodes, therapies) from timestamped clinical data and background knowledge, while handling inconsistencies through constraint-based repair.
AI.method: Uses logical rules to define existence/termination conditions for simple events and combines them into meta-events. Applies constraints to identify incompatible event combinations with a repair mechanism for preferred consistent sets. Implemented via answer set programming.
AI.result: Evaluation on a lung cancer use case shows computational feasibility and strong alignment with medical expert opinions.
AI.conclusion: The framework is effective for healthcare event detection and is generic enough for reuse in other domains.

title: Seeing Isn't Believing: Uncovering Blind Spots in Evaluator Vision-Language Models
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly used to evaluate outputs of other models, for image-to-text (I2T) tasks such as visual question answering, and text-to-image (T2I) generation tasks. Despite this growing reliance, the reliability of these Evaluator VLMs remains under explored. In this work, we systematically evaluate the reliability of Evaluator VLMs across both I2T and T2I tasks. We introduce targeted perturbations that degrade output quality along key error dimensions, including object hallucinations, spatial reasoning, factual grounding, and visual fidelity. These perturbations test whether Evaluator VLMs can reliably account for these quality degrading errors in their evaluations. Using a comprehensive benchmark of over 4000 perturbed instances spanning 40 perturbation dimensions, we evaluate 4 prominent VLMs using single-answer scoring, pairwise comparison, and reference-guided paradigms. Our findings reveal that current VLM evaluators exhibit substantial blind spots: they often fail to detect perturbed outputs - in some cases exceeding 50%, struggle particularly with fine-grained compositional and spatial errors, and are often insensitive to hallucinated content that contradicts the input image. Pairwise comparison proves more reliable, though failure rates persist. These results highlight the unreliable nature of current Evaluator VLMs and urge caution in their deployment for benchmarking and development decisions. Code and data have been made publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates the reliability of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) used as evaluators for image-to-text and text-to-image tasks, finding they have substantial blind spots—failing to detect perturbed outputs over 50% of the time in some cases—and urges caution in their deployment.
AI.motivation: VLMs are increasingly used as evaluators for other models' outputs in I2T and T2I tasks, yet their reliability as evaluators has been under-explored, motivating a systematic investigation.
AI.method: The authors introduced targeted perturbations along key error dimensions (object hallucinations, spatial reasoning, factual grounding, visual fidelity) and evaluated 4 prominent VLMs using 4000+ perturbed instances across 40 perturbation dimensions, employing single-answer scoring, pairwise comparison, and reference-guided paradigms.
AI.result: Current VLM evaluators exhibit substantial blind spots, failing to detect perturbed outputs (exceeding 50% in some cases), struggling with fine-grained compositional and spatial errors, and being insensitive to hallucinated content. Pairwise comparison is more reliable but failure rates persist.
AI.conclusion: Current Evaluator VLMs are unreliable for benchmarking and development decisions, urging caution in their deployment.

title: Conditional anomaly detection with soft harmonic functions
comment: Published at IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM 2011). 10.1109/ICDM.2011.40
summary: In this paper, we consider the problem of conditional anomaly detection that aims to identify data instances with an unusual response or a class label. We develop a new non-parametric approach for conditional anomaly detection based on the soft harmonic solution, with which we estimate the confidence of the label to detect anomalous mislabeling. We further regularize the solution to avoid the detection of isolated examples and examples on the boundary of the distribution support. We demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method on several synthetic and UCI ML datasets in detecting unusual labels when compared to several baseline approaches. We also evaluate the performance of our method on a real-world electronic health record dataset where we seek to identify unusual patient-management decisions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new non-parametric conditional anomaly detection method using a soft harmonic solution to identify instances with unusual labels, with regularization to avoid detecting boundary or isolated examples, and demonstrates effectiveness on synthetic, UCI, and real-world EHR datasets.
AI.motivation: To develop a method for conditional anomaly detection that identifies data instances with unusual responses or class labels, particularly addressing the limitation of detecting isolated examples and boundary instances.
AI.method: A non-parametric approach based on the soft harmonic solution that estimates label confidence for detecting anomalous mislabeling, with regularization to avoid false detection of isolated or boundary examples.
AI.result: The method outperforms baseline approaches on synthetic and UCI ML datasets for detecting unusual labels, and shows effectiveness on a real-world electronic health record dataset for identifying unusual patient-management decisions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed soft harmonic solution-based approach is effective for conditional anomaly detection of unusual labels across multiple domains, including healthcare.

title: Learning to Communicate: Toward End-to-End Optimization of Multi-Agent Language Systems
summary: Multi-agent systems built on large language models have shown strong performance on complex reasoning tasks, yet most work focuses on agent roles and orchestration while treating inter-agent communication as a fixed interface. Latent communication through internal representations such as key-value caches offers a promising alternative to text-based protocols, but existing approaches do not jointly optimize communication with multi-agent reasoning. Therefore we propose DiffMAS, a training framework that treats latent communication as a learnable component of multi-agent systems. DiffMAS performs parameter-efficient supervised training over multi-agent latent trajectories, enabling agents to jointly learn how information should be encoded and interpreted across interactions. Experiments on mathematical reasoning, scientific QA, code generation, and commonsense benchmarks show that DiffMAS consistently improves reasoning accuracy and decoding stability over single-agent inference, text-based multi-agent systems, and prior latent communication methods, achieving 26.7% on AIME24, 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond, and consistent gains across reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DiffMAS is a training framework that treats latent communication (via key-value caches) as a learnable component in multi-agent LLM systems, jointly optimizing how information is encoded and interpreted across agents, achieving significant gains on math, science QA, code generation, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM systems treat inter-agent communication as a fixed text-based interface and do not jointly optimize communication with multi-agent reasoning. Latent communication via internal representations is promising but lacks a training framework to learn it.
AI.method: DiffMAS performs parameter-efficient supervised training over multi-agent latent trajectories, enabling agents to jointly learn how to encode and interpret information across interactions using latent communication (key-value caches) rather than fixed text protocols.
AI.result: Achieves 26.7% on AIME24, 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond, and consistent gains across math reasoning, scientific QA, code generation, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks, outperforming single-agent inference, text-based multi-agent systems, and prior latent communication methods.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing latent communication with multi-agent reasoning significantly improves both reasoning accuracy and decoding stability, suggesting that learning how agents communicate is as important as what they communicate.

title: Attention-based multiple instance learning for predominant growth pattern prediction in lung adenocarcinoma wsi using foundation models
summary: Lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) grading depends on accurately identifying growth patterns, which are indicators of prognosis and can influence treatment decisions. Common deep learning approaches to determine the predominant pattern rely on patch-level classification or segmentation, requiring extensive annotations. This study proposes an attention-based multiple instance learning (ABMIL) framework to predict the predominant LUAD growth pattern at the whole slide level to reduce annotation burden. Our approach integrates pretrained pathology foundation models as patch encoders, used either frozen or fine-tuned on annotated patches, to extract discriminative features that are aggregated through attention mechanisms. Experiments show that fine-tuned encoders improve performance, with Prov-GigaPath achieving the highest agreement (\k{appa} = 0.699) under ABMIL. Compared to simple patch-aggregation baselines, ABMIL yields more robust predictions by leveraging slide-level supervision and spatial attention. Future work will extend this framework to estimate the full distribution of growth patterns and validate performance on external cohorts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an attention-based multiple instance learning (ABMIL) framework that uses pretrained pathology foundation models as patch encoders to predict the predominant lung adenocarcinoma growth pattern at the whole slide level, reducing the need for extensive annotations. Fine-tuned encoders (especially Prov-GigaPath) achieved best agreement (kappa=0.699) and outperformed simple patch-aggregation baselines.
AI.motivation: To reduce the annotation burden in LUAD grading by moving from patch-level classification/segmentation to slide-level prediction using weakly supervised learning.
AI.method: An ABMIL framework integrating pretrained pathology foundation models (frozen or fine-tuned) as patch encoders, with attention mechanisms for aggregating patch features at the whole slide level.
AI.result: Fine-tuned encoders outperformed frozen ones; Prov-GigaPath achieved highest agreement (kappa=0.699) under ABMIL; ABMIL yielded more robust predictions than simple patch-aggregation baselines.
AI.conclusion: ABMIL with fine-tuned pathology foundation models is effective for slide-level LUAD growth pattern prediction, and future work will extend to full pattern distribution estimation and external validation.

title: Dynamical Priors as a Training Objective in Reinforcement Learning
comment: Supplementary material can be accessed here: https://github.com/drsukeshs/esd-rl
summary: Standard reinforcement learning (RL) optimizes policies for reward but imposes few constraints on how decisions evolve over time. As a result, policies may achieve high performance while exhibiting temporally incoherent behavior such as abrupt confidence shifts, oscillations, or degenerate inactivity. We introduce Dynamical Prior Reinforcement Learning (DP-RL), a training framework that augments policy gradient learning with an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics that implement evidence accumulation and hysteresis. Without modifying the reward, environment, or policy architecture, this prior shapes the temporal evolution of action probabilities during learning. Across three minimal environments, we show that dynamical priors systematically alter decision trajectories in task-dependent ways, promoting temporally structured behavior that cannot be explained by generic smoothing. These results demonstrate that training objectives alone can control the temporal geometry of decision-making in RL agents.

AI.tldr: DP-RL augments policy gradient learning with an auxiliary loss from state dynamics (evidence accumulation & hysteresis) to shape temporal decision structure, producing coherent behavior beyond generic smoothing.
AI.motivation: Standard RL policies can achieve high reward while exhibiting temporally incoherent behavior (abrupt shifts, oscillations, inactivity); the authors aim to impose temporal structure without modifying reward, environment, or policy architecture.
AI.method: DP-RL adds an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics (evidence accumulation and hysteresis) to policy gradient learning, shaping the temporal evolution of action probabilities during training.
AI.result: In three minimal environments, dynamical priors systematically alter decision trajectories in task-dependent ways, promoting temporally structured behavior not explainable by generic smoothing.
AI.conclusion: Training objectives alone can control the temporal geometry of decision-making in RL agents.

title: Tool Attention Is All You Need: Dynamic Tool Gating and Lazy Schema Loading for Eliminating the MCP/Tools Tax in Scalable Agentic Workflows
summary: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has become a common interface for connecting large language model (LLM) agents to external tools, but its reliance on stateless, eager schema injection imposes a hidden per-turn overhead the MCP Tax or Tools Tax that practitioner reports place between roughly 10k and 60k tokens in typical multi-server deployments. This payload inflates the key-value cache, is associated with reasoning degradation as context utilization approaches published fracture points around 70%, and turns token budgets into a recurring operational cost. We introduce Tool Attention, a middleware-layer mechanism that generalizes the "Attention Is All You Need" paradigm from self-attention over tokens to gated attention over tools. Tool Attention combines (i) an Intent Schema Overlap (ISO) score from sentence embeddings, (ii) a state-aware gating function enforcing preconditions and access scopes, and (iii) a two-phase lazy schema loader that keeps a compact summary pool in context and promotes full JSON schemas only for top-k gated tools. We evaluate on a simulated 120-tool, six-server benchmark whose per-server token counts are calibrated to public audits of real MCP deployments. In this simulation, Tool Attention directly reduces measured per-turn tool tokens by 95.0% (47.3k -> 2.4k) and raises effective context utilization (a token-ratio quantity) from 24% to 91%. End-to-end figures for task success, latency, cost, and reasoning quality are reported as projections derived from the measured token counts combined with published deployment telemetry; they are not measured on live LLM agents, and we mark projected values explicitly throughout. Taken together, the results support a simple thesis: protocol-level efficiency, not raw context length, is a binding constraint on scalable gentic systems. The code for this work is accessible at https://github.com/asadani/tool-attention

AI.tldr: Introduces Tool Attention, a middleware mechanism that reduces per-turn tool token overhead (the "Tools Tax") from ~47k to ~2.4k tokens in multi-server MCP deployments, achieving 95% reduction via gated attention over tools combined with lazy schema loading.
AI.motivation: MCP's stateless, eager schema injection causes a hidden per-turn overhead of 10k-60k tokens in multi-server deployments, inflating KV cache, degrading reasoning near 70% context utilization, and turning token budgets into recurring operational costs.
AI.method: Tool Attention combines (i) Intent Schema Overlap (ISO) scores from sentence embeddings, (ii) state-aware gating for preconditions/access scopes, and (iii) a two-phase lazy schema loader that keeps compact summary pools and promotes full JSON schemas only for top-k gated tools.
AI.result: In a simulated 120-tool, six-server benchmark calibrated to real MCP audits: 95% reduction in per-turn tool tokens (47.3k→2.4k), effective context utilization improved from 24% to 91%. End-to-end metrics reported as projections from token counts and published telemetry.
AI.conclusion: Protocol-level efficiency, not raw context length, is the binding constraint on scalable agentic systems. Tool Attention demonstrates that gated, lazy tool selection can dramatically reduce overhead without sacrificing functionality.

title: Component-Based Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection requires sensitivity to subtle shifts without overreacting to natural In-Distribution (ID) diversity. However, from the viewpoint of detection granularity, global representation inevitably suppress local OOD cues, while patch-based methods are unstable due to entangled spurious-correlation and noise. And neither them is effective in detecting compositional OODs composed of valid ID components. Inspired by recognition-by-components theory, we present a training-free Component-Based OOD Detection (CoOD) framework that addresses the existing limitations by decomposing inputs into functional components. To instantiate CoOD, we derive Component Shift Score (CSS) to detect local appearance shifts, and Compositional Consistency Score (CCS) to identify cross-component compositional inconsistencies. Empirically, CoOD achieves consistent improvements on both coarse- and fine-grained OOD detection.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CoOD, a training-free framework for OOD detection that decomposes images into functional components to detect both local appearance shifts and compositional inconsistencies, outperforming existing methods on coarse- and fine-grained OOD benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing OOD detection methods struggle because global representations suppress local OOD cues, patch-based methods are unstable due to spurious correlations/noise, and neither effectively detects compositional OODs (where valid ID components are combined abnormally).
AI.method: Inspired by recognition-by-components theory, CoOD decomposes inputs into functional components. It uses Component Shift Score (CSS) for local appearance shifts and Compositional Consistency Score (CCS) for cross-component compositional inconsistencies. The framework is training-free.
AI.result: CoOD achieves consistent improvements on both coarse-grained and fine-grained OOD detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: Component-level decomposition offers a promising direction for OOD detection by addressing limitations of both global and patch-based methods, particularly for compositional OOD scenarios.

title: Drug Synergy Prediction via Residual Graph Isomorphism Networks and Attention Mechanisms
summary: In the treatment of complex diseases, treatment regimens using a single drug often yield limited efficacy and can lead to drug resistance. In contrast, combination drug therapies can significantly improve therapeutic outcomes through synergistic effects. However, experimentally validating all possible drug combinations is prohibitively expensive, underscoring the critical need for efficient computational prediction methods. Although existing approaches based on deep learning and graph neural networks (GNNs) have made considerable progress, challenges remain in reducing structural bias, improving generalization capability, and enhancing model interpretability. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a collaborative prediction graph neural network that integrates molecular structural features and cell-line genomic profiles with drug-drug interactions to enhance the prediction of synergistic effects. We introduce a novel model named the Residual Graph Isomorphism Network integrated with an Attention mechanism (ResGIN-Att). The model first extracts multi scale topological features of drug molecules using a residual graph isomorphism network, where residual connections help mitigate over-smoothing in deep layers. Subsequently, an adaptive Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) module fuses structural information from local to global scales. Finally, a cross-attention module is designed to explicitly model drug-drug interactions and identify key chemical substructures. Extensive experiments on five public benchmark datasets demonstrate that ResGIN-Att achieves competitive performance, comparing favorably against key baseline methods while exhibiting promising generalization capability and robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ResGIN-Att, a graph neural network model that integrates molecular structures, cell-line genomic profiles, and drug-drug interactions to predict synergistic effects of drug combinations, achieving competitive performance on five benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Single-drug treatments for complex diseases have limited efficacy and risk drug resistance, while experimentally validating all possible drug combinations is too expensive, creating a need for efficient computational prediction methods.
AI.method: ResGIN-Att uses a residual graph isomorphism network with residual connections to extract multi-scale topological features, an adaptive LSTM for local-to-global fusion, and a cross-attention module to model drug-drug interactions and identify key substructures.
AI.result: The model achieves competitive performance across five public benchmark datasets, showing strong generalization capability and robustness compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: ResGIN-Att effectively addresses limitations in existing deep learning approaches for drug synergy prediction, including structural bias, generalization, and interpretability issues.

title: Alignment has a Fantasia Problem
summary: Modern AI assistants are trained to follow instructions, implicitly assuming that users can clearly articulate their goals and the kind of assistance they need. Decades of behavioral research, however, show that people often engage with AI systems before their goals are fully formed. When AI systems treat prompts as complete expressions of intent, they can appear to be useful or convenient, but not necessarily aligned with the users' needs. We call these failures Fantasia interactions. We argue that Fantasia interactions demand a rethinking of alignment research: rather than treating users as rational oracles, AI should provide cognitive support by actively helping users form and refine their intent through time. This requires an interdisciplinary approach that bridges machine learning, interface design, and behavioral science. We synthesize insights from these fields to characterize the mechanisms and failures of Fantasia interactions. We then show why existing interventions are insufficient, and propose a research agenda for designing and evaluating AI systems that better help humans navigate uncertainty in their tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies "Fantasia interactions"—where users engage AI before fully forming their goals—arguing that current alignment research wrongly treats users as rational oracles, and proposes an interdisciplinary research agenda for AI systems that actively help users refine their intent.
AI.motivation: Behavioral research shows people often engage AI before their goals are fully formed, but current AI systems treat prompts as complete expressions of intent, leading to misalignment with users' actual needs.
AI.method: The paper synthesizes insights from machine learning, interface design, and behavioral science to characterize the mechanisms and failures of Fantasia interactions, then evaluates why existing interventions are insufficient.
AI.result: Identifies that Fantasia interactions are a distinct failure mode of alignment, and shows that current approaches (treating users as rational oracles) are inadequate to address them.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a research agenda for designing and evaluating AI systems that provide cognitive support to help users form and refine their intent through time, requiring interdisciplinary approaches.

title: Deep kernel video approximation for unsupervised action segmentation
summary: This work focuses on per-video unsupervised action segmentation, which is of interest to applications where storing large datasets is either not possible, or nor permitted. We propose to segment videos by learning in deep kernel space, to approximate the underlying frame distribution, as closely as possible. To define this closeness metric between the original video distribution and its approximation, we rely on maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) which is a geometry-preserving metric in distribution space, and thus gives more reliable estimates. Moreover, unlike the commonly used optimal transport metric, MMD is both easier to optimize, and faster. We choose to use neural tangent kernels (NTKs) to define the kernel space where MMD operates, because of their improved descriptive power as opposed to fixed kernels. And, also, because NTKs sidestep the trivial solution, when jointly learning the inputs (video approximation) and the kernel function. Finally, we show competitive results when compared to state-of-the-art per-video methods, on six standard benchmarks. Additionally, our method has higher F1 scores than prior agglomerative work, when the number of segments is unknown.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a per-video unsupervised action segmentation method using neural tangent kernels and maximum mean discrepancy to approximate video frame distributions without requiring large datasets.
AI.motivation: To enable unsupervised action segmentation in scenarios where storing large datasets is impossible or not permitted, addressing the need for per-video methods that work without extensive training data.
AI.method: The approach learns in deep kernel space using Neural Tangent Kernels (NTKs) within a Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) framework to approximate the underlying frame distribution. MMD provides a geometry-preserving metric that is easier to optimize and faster than optimal transport. NTKs offer improved descriptive power over fixed kernels and avoid trivial solutions when jointly learning inputs and kernel functions.
AI.result: Competitive results on six standard benchmarks compared to state-of-the-art per-video methods, with higher F1 scores than prior agglomerative work when the number of segments is unknown.
AI.conclusion: The proposed NTK + MMD approach is effective for per-video unsupervised action segmentation, offering both performance advantages and practical benefits in data-constrained settings.

title: Generalizing Numerical Reasoning in Table Data through Operation Sketches and Self-Supervised Learning
comment: Accepted to TACL. This is a pre-MIT Press publication version
summary: Numerical reasoning over expert-domain tables often exhibits high in-domain accuracy but limited robustness to domain shift. Models trained with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on specific datasets tend to rely on header-operation shortcuts rather than structural reasoning. We introduce TaNOS, a continual pre-training framework comprising three components: (i) header anonymization to reduce lexical memorization, (ii) operation sketches that provide minimal structural cues, and (iii) self-supervised pretraining that constructs correctness-guaranteed program-question pairs from given tables in a program-first manner. By decoupling domain semantics and numerical operation structure, TaNOS improves the transferability of numerical reasoning. Applied to an 8B instruction-tuned model, TaNOS achieves 80.13% execution accuracy on FinQA with only 10% train data, outperforming SFT baseline (73.97%) with full train data and proprietary models such as GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro. Furthermore, in the domain-shift experiments, TaNOS displays nearly-negligible cross-domain gap (<2pp) when standard SFT shows over 10pp gap. These results suggest that structural guidance with operation sketches, header-agnostic representations, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision can improve the robustness of numerical reasoning across diverse expert-domain tables.

AI.tldr: TaNOS is a continual pre-training framework that improves numerical reasoning robustness across expert-domain tables by using header anonymization, operation sketches, and self-supervised pretraining, achieving 80.13% accuracy on FinQA with only 10% training data and near-negligible domain shift gaps.
AI.motivation: Numerical reasoning over expert-domain tables suffers from poor robustness to domain shift because models trained with SFT rely on header-operation shortcuts rather than structural reasoning.
AI.method: TaNOS has three components: (1) header anonymization to reduce lexical memorization, (2) operation sketches providing minimal structural cues, and (3) self-supervised pretraining that builds correctness-guaranteed program-question pairs from tables in a program-first manner.
AI.result: TaNOS achieves 80.13% execution accuracy on FinQA with only 10% training data, outperforming SFT baseline (73.97%) with full data and proprietary models (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro). In domain-shift experiments, TaNOS shows <2pp cross-domain gap vs. >10pp for standard SFT.
AI.conclusion: Structural guidance with operation sketches, header-agnostic representations, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision improve the robustness and transferability of numerical reasoning across diverse expert-domain tables.

title: Bounding the Black Box: A Statistical Certification Framework for AI Risk Regulation
summary:
Artificial intelligence now decides who receives a loan, who is flagged for criminal investigation, and whether an autonomous vehicle brakes in time. Governments have responded: the EU AI Act, the NIST Risk Management Framework, and the Council of Europe Convention all demand that high-risk systems demonstrate safety before deployment. Yet beneath this regulatory consensus lies a critical vacuum: none specifies what ``acceptable risk'' means in quantitative terms, and none provides a technical method for verifying that a deployed system actually meets such a threshold. The regulatory architecture is in place; the verification instrument is not.
  This gap is not theoretical. As the EU AI Act moves into full enforcement, developers face mandatory conformity assessments without established methodologies for producing quantitative safety evidence - and the systems most in need of oversight are opaque statistical inference engines that resist white-box scrutiny.
  This paper provides the missing instrument. Drawing on the aviation certification paradigm, we propose a two-stage framework that transforms AI risk regulation into engineering practice. In Stage One, a competent authority formally fixes an acceptable failure probability $δ$ and an operational input domain $\varepsilon$ - a normative act with direct civil liability implications. In Stage Two, the RoMA and gRoMA statistical verification tools compute a definitive, auditable upper bound on the system's true failure rate, requiring no access to model internals and scaling to arbitrary architectures. We demonstrate how this certificate satisfies existing regulatory obligations, shifts accountability upstream to developers, and integrates with the legal frameworks that exist today.

AI.tldr: Existing AI regulations lack quantitative definitions of acceptable risk and technical verification methods. This paper bridges that gap by proposing a two-stage framework (inspired by aviation certification) using RoMA/gRoMA statistical tools to compute auditable failure-rate bounds, enabling developers to produce verifiable safety evidence for high-risk AI systems.
AI.motivation: Regulatory frameworks (EU AI Act, NIST RMF, Council of Europe Convention) demand safety demonstration for high-risk AI but fail to define "acceptable risk" quantitatively or provide technical verification methods. This creates a critical implementation gap as enforcement approaches.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) A competent authority sets an acceptable failure probability δ and operational input domain ε. (2) RoMA/gRoMA statistical verification tools compute an auditable upper bound on the system's true failure rate, requiring no white-box access and scaling to arbitrary architectures.
AI.result: The framework produces a definitive, auditable certificate of the system's failure-rate upper bound, satisfying existing regulatory obligations, shifting accountability to developers, and integrating with current legal frameworks.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the missing verification instrument for AI risk regulation, transforming regulatory requirements into engineering practice by drawing on aviation certification principles.

title: CHRep: Cross-modal Histology Representation and Post-hoc Calibration for Spatial Gene Expression Prediction
summary: Spatial transcriptomics (ST) enables spatially resolved gene profiling but remains expensive and low-throughput, limiting large-cohort studies and routine clinical use. Predicting spatial gene expression from routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) slides is a promising alternative, yet under realistic leave-one-slide-out evaluation, existing models often suffer from slide-level appearance shifts and regression-driven over-smoothing that suppress biologically meaningful variation. CHRep is a two-phase framework for robust histology-to-expression prediction. In the training phase, CHRep learns a structure-aware representation by jointly optimizing correlation-aware regression, symmetric image-expression alignment, and coordinate-induced spatial topology regularization. In the inference phase, cross-slide robustness is improved without backbone fine-tuning through a lightweight calibration module trained on the training slides, which combines a non-parametric estimate from a training gallery with a magnitude-regularized correction module. Unlike prior embedding-alignment or retrieval-based transfer methods that rely on a single prediction route, CHRep couples topology-preserving representation learning with post-hoc calibration, enabling stable neighborhood retrieval and controlled bias correction under slide-level shifts. Across the three cohorts, CHRep consistently improves gene-wise correlation under leave-one-slide-out evaluation, with the largest gains observed on Alex+10x. Relative to HAGE, the Pearson correlation coefficient on all considered genes [PCC(ACG)] increases by 4.0% on cSCC and 9.8% on HER2+. Relative to mclSTExp, PCC(ACG) further improves by 39.5% on Alex+10x, together with 9.7% and 9.0% reductions in mean squared error (MSE) and mean absolute error (MAE), respectively.

AI.tldr: CHRep is a two-phase framework for predicting spatial gene expression from H&E slides. It uses structure-aware representation learning (correlation-aware regression, image-expression alignment, spatial topology regularization) during training, and a lightweight post-hoc calibration module at inference to handle slide-level appearance shifts. It outperforms existing methods across three cohorts, e.g., 39.5% PCC improvement on Alex+10x over mclSTExp.
AI.motivation: Spatial transcriptomics is expensive and low-throughput, limiting large-cohort studies. Predicting gene expression from routine H&E slides is a promising alternative, but existing models suffer from slide-level appearance shifts and regression-driven over-smoothing that suppress biologically meaningful variation.
AI.method: CHRep is a two-phase framework. Training phase: learns structure-aware representations via (1) correlation-aware regression, (2) symmetric image-expression alignment, and (3) coordinate-induced spatial topology regularization. Inference phase: a lightweight calibration module (non-parametric gallery retrieval + magnitude-regularized correction) improves cross-slide robustness without backbone fine-tuning.
AI.result: Across three cohorts, CHRep consistently improves gene-wise correlation under leave-one-slide-out evaluation. Largest gains on Alex+10x: 39.5% PCC improvement over mclSTExp, with 9.7% MSE and 9.0% MAE reductions. Relative to HAGE: +4.0% PCC on cSCC, +9.8% on HER2+.
AI.conclusion: CHRep couples topology-preserving representation learning with post-hoc calibration, enabling stable neighborhood retrieval and controlled bias correction under slide-level shifts, offering a robust and practical alternative to costly spatial transcriptomics.

title: A temporal deep learning framework for calibration of low-cost air quality sensors
summary: Low-cost air quality sensors (LCS) provide a practical alternative to expensive regulatory-grade instruments, making dense urban monitoring networks possible. Yet their adoption is limited by calibration challenges, including sensor drift, environmental cross-sensitivity, and variability in performance from device to device. This work presents a deep learning framework for calibrating LCS measurements of PM$_{2.5}$, PM$_{10}$, and NO$_2$ using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, trained on co-located reference data from the OxAria network in Oxford, UK. Unlike the Random Forest (RF) baseline, which treats each observation independently, the proposed approach captures temporal dependencies and delayed environmental effects through sequence-based learning, achieving higher $R^2$ values across training, validation, and test sets for all three pollutants. A feature set is constructed combining time-lagged parameters, harmonic encodings, and interaction terms to improve generalization on unseen temporal windows. Validation of unseen calibrated values against the Equivalence Spreadsheet Tool 3.1 demonstrates regulatory compliance with expanded uncertainties of 22.11% for NO$_2$, 12.42% for PM$_{10}$, and 9.1% for PM$_{2.5}$.

AI.tldr: An LSTM-based deep learning framework calibrates low-cost air quality sensors (PM2.5, PM10, NO2) using temporal dependencies, outperforming Random Forest baselines and achieving regulatory compliance with low expanded uncertainties.
AI.motivation: Low-cost air quality sensors (LCS) face calibration challenges—drift, cross-sensitivity, device variability—limiting their adoption despite enabling dense urban monitoring networks.
AI.method: LSTM network trained on co-located reference data from OxAria network (Oxford, UK) with time-lagged parameters, harmonic encodings, and interaction terms; compared against Random Forest baseline.
AI.result: Higher R² values across all pollutants vs. RF; regulatory compliance with expanded uncertainties: NO₂ 22.11%, PM₁₀ 12.42%, PM₂.₅ 9.1%.
AI.conclusion: Sequence-based LSTM calibration effectively addresses LCS limitations, achieving regulatory-grade performance for urban air quality monitoring.

title: Nemobot Games: Crafting Strategic AI Gaming Agents for Interactive Learning with Large Language Models
summary: This paper introduces a new paradigm for AI game programming, leveraging large language models (LLMs) to extend and operationalize Claude Shannon's taxonomy of game-playing machines. Central to this paradigm is Nemobot, an interactive agentic engineering environment that enables users to create, customize, and deploy LLM-powered game agents while actively engaging with AI-driven strategies. The LLM-based chatbot, integrated within Nemobot, demonstrates its capabilities across four distinct classes of games. For dictionary-based games, it compresses state-action mappings into efficient, generalized models for rapid adaptability. In rigorously solvable games, it employs mathematical reasoning to compute optimal strategies and generates human-readable explanations for its decisions. For heuristic-based games, it synthesizes strategies by combining insights from classical minimax algorithms (see, e.g., shannon1950chess) with crowd-sourced data. Finally, in learning-based games, it utilizes reinforcement learning with human feedback and self-critique to iteratively refine strategies through trial-and-error and imitation learning. Nemobot amplifies this framework by offering a programmable environment where users can experiment with tool-augmented generation and fine-tuning of strategic game agents. From strategic games to role-playing games, Nemobot demonstrates how AI agents can achieve a form of self-programming by integrating crowdsourced learning and human creativity to iteratively refine their own logic. This represents a step toward the long-term goal of self-programming AI.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Nemobot, an interactive engineering environment powered by LLMs, that operationalizes Shannon's taxonomy of game-playing machines across four game classes (dictionary-based, solvable, heuristic-based, and learning-based games), enabling users to create and customize AI game agents that achieve a form of self-programming through crowdsourced learning and human feedback.
AI.motivation: To extend and operationalize Claude Shannon's taxonomy of game-playing machines using large language models, enabling a new paradigm for AI game programming where agents can achieve self-programming capabilities.
AI.method: Nemobot, an interactive agentic engineering environment that integrates LLM-based chatbots with tool-augmented generation and fine-tuning, addressing four game classes: (1) dictionary-based games via compressed state-action mappings, (2) solvable games via mathematical reasoning with explainable outputs, (3) heuristic-based games via combining minimax algorithms with crowdsourced data, and (4) learning-based games via reinforcement learning with human feedback and self-critique.
AI.result: Nemobot demonstrates successful application across all four game classes, showing how AI agents can integrate crowdsourced learning and human creativity to iteratively refine their logic, moving toward self-programming AI.
AI.conclusion: The work represents a meaningful step toward self-programming AI by showing how LLM-powered agents can iteratively refine their own strategies through human feedback and crowdsourced learning within an interactive engineering environment.

title: OmniFit: Multi-modal 3D Body Fitting via Scale-agnostic Dense Landmark Prediction
summary: Fitting an underlying body model to 3D clothed human assets has been extensively studied, yet most approaches focus on either single-modal inputs such as point clouds or multi-view images alone, often requiring a known metric scale. This constraint is frequently impractical, especially for AI-generated assets where scale distortion is common. We propose OmniFit, a method that can seamlessly handle diverse multi-modal inputs, including full scans, partial depth observations, and image captures, while remaining scale-agnostic for both real and synthetic assets. Our key innovation is a simple yet effective conditional transformer decoder that directly maps surface points to dense body landmarks, which are then used for SMPL-X parameter fitting. In addition, an optional plug-and-play image adapter incorporates visual cues to compensate for missing geometric information. We further introduce a dedicated scale predictor that rescales subjects to canonical body proportions. OmniFit substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 57.1 to 80.9 percent across daily and loose clothing scenarios. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first body fitting method to surpass multi-view optimization baselines and the first to achieve millimeter-level accuracy on the CAPE and 4D-DRESS benchmarks.

AI.tldr: OmniFit is a scale-agnostic body fitting method that handles diverse multi-modal inputs (scans, depth, images) using a conditional transformer decoder to map surface points to SMPL-X landmarks, achieving state-of-the-art results with 57.1-80.9% improvement over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing body fitting methods focus on single-modal inputs (point clouds or multi-view images) and require known metric scale, which is impractical for AI-generated assets with scale distortion. The goal is to create a method that handles diverse multi-modal inputs while being scale-agnostic.
AI.method: Uses a conditional transformer decoder to map surface points to dense body landmarks for SMPL-X parameter fitting, plus an optional plug-and-play image adapter for visual cues and a dedicated scale predictor to rescale subjects to canonical body proportions.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 57.1-80.9% across daily and loose clothing scenarios. First body fitting method to surpass multi-view optimization baselines and achieve millimeter-level accuracy on CAPE and 4D-DRESS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: OmniFit is effective for diverse multi-modal inputs, scale-agnostic, and sets new benchmarks in body fitting accuracy for both real and synthetic assets.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Coupled Demand Forecasting and Supply Chain Optimization
comment: The paper is accepted in the Computers, Materials & Continua journal
summary: Supply chain resilience and efficiency are vital in industries characterized by volatile demand and uncertain supply, such as textiles and personal protective equipment (PPE). Traditional forecasting and optimization approaches often operate in isolation, limiting their real-world effectiveness.
